title: above

messages.description: when left inlet value rises above the threshold value
when left inlet falls back to or below the threshold

ELSE/above.body: [above] sends a bang to the left outlet when the left inlet is above the threshold value. Conversely, it sends a bang to the right outlet when the left inlet falls back to the threshold value or below it. The threshold value can be set via the argument or the 2nd inlet.

title: above~

messages.description: when left inlet value rises above the threshold
when left inlet falls back below the threshold

ELSE/above~.body: [above~] sends an impulse to the left outlet when the left inlet is above the threshold value. Conversely, it outputs an impulse to its right outlet when the left inlet falls back to the threshold value or below it. The threshold value can be set via the argument or the 2nd inlet.

title: abs.pd~

messages.description: the left input to pd~ subprocess
the right input to pd~ subprocess

methods.description: start a subprocess, no symbol restarts last one

ELSE/abs.pd~.body: [abs.pd~] loads a pd file into a subprocess via [pd~] and makes things more convenient like making the subprocess able to receive MIDI data from MIDI devices connected to the parent process (thanks to [sendmidi]). Also, you can open the subprocess patch by clicking on the object.

title: add
ELSE/add.body: [add] starts accumulating input values to a starting sum value (0 by default). The starting sum can be set as an argument or via the 'set' message. You can reset the sum to the starting sum value with a bang to the right inlet.

title: add~
ELSE/add~.body: [add~] starts accumulating input values to a starting sum value (0 by default). The starting sum can be set as an argument or via the 'set' message. You can reset the sum to the starting sum value with a bang or with an impulse signal to the right inlet.

title: adsr~

messages.description: gate value in MIDI velocity range (0-127 is 0-1)
signal retrigger via impulses and gates

flags.name: -lin
flags.description:
sets to linear mode
default: log

methods.description: nonzero sets to immediate release mode

ELSE/adsr~.body: [adsr~] is an attack/decay/sustain/release gated envelope. At gate on (transition from 0 to any value), the attack ramp is generated, then a decay ramp goes to the sustain point (specified as a ratio of the gate value). At gate off (transition from any value to 0), [adsr~] goes to 0 at the specified release time in ms.

flags.name: -bw
flags.description: sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

methods.description: clears filter's memory if you blow it up
sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

title: allpass.filt~
ELSE/allpass.filt~.body: [allpass.filt~] is an allpass filter whose order can be set via the first argument (must be a multiple of 2). This is an abstraction that stacks many [allpass.2nd~] objects in cascade.

title: allpass.rev~

arguments.description: maximum and initial delay time in ms

methods.description: changes the max delay size (in ms)

ELSE/allpass.rev~.body: [allpass.rev~] serves as a reverberator, allpass filter and delay. By default, you can set a delay time and a reverberation/decay time in ms ("t60"), which is the time the impulse takes to fall 60dB in energy (but you can change this parameter to a gain coefficient).

title: amean
ELSE/amean.body: [amean] creates a list of arithmetic means. It takes a start point, an end point and the number of steps from start to end. The number of steps is the output list length -1 and represents the number of values until reaching the end. The minimum number of steps is "1".

title: any2symbol

messages.description: any message to be converted to a symbol

ELSE/any2symbol.body: [any2symbol] converts any message to a symbol message.

title: args

messages.description: arguments given in the parent patch (bang is empty list)

ELSE/args.body: [args] loads arguments of an abstraction and can also change them. This is useful for the management of a variable number of arguments in abstractions, and can be used to change/set them up.

title: asr~

messages.description: gate values in MIDI velocity range (0-127 is 0-1)
trigger at control rate (like an impulse)

flags.name: -lin
flags.description:
sets to linear mode
default: log

methods.description: nonzero sets to immediate release mode

ELSE/asr~.body: [asr~] is an attack/sustain/release gated envelope. At gate on (transition from 0 to any value), [asr~] goes from 0 to the gate value at the specified attack time in ms. At gate off (transition from any value to 0) [asr~] goes to 0 at the specified release time in ms.

title: autofade.mc~

messages.description: input signals to be faded in/out

arguments.description: fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, lin, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

methods.description: fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

ELSE/autofade.mc~.body: [autofade.mc~] is an automatic fade in/out for multichanne inputs. It responds to a gate control and uses internal lookup tables just like [fader~]. A gate on happens when the last input value was zero or less and the incoming value isn't. A gate off happens when the last value was greater than zero value and the incoming value isn't! The maximum gain depends on the gate on level.

title: autofade2.mc~
ELSE/autofade2.mc~.description: automatic fade with indepent in/out times

messages.description: input signals to be faded in/out

arguments.description: fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, lin, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

methods.description: fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

ELSE/autofade2.mc~.body: [autofade2.mc~] is an automatic fade in/out for multichanne inputs. It responds to a gate control and uses internal lookup tables just like [fader~]. A gate on happens when the last input value was zero or less and the incoming value isn't. A gate off happens when the last value was greater than zero value and the incoming value isn't! The maximum gain depends on the gate on level.

title: autofade2~

messages.description: input signal to be faded in/out
fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>
input signal to be faded in/out
a 1 or 0 is sent when the fade in/out is finished

arguments.description: (optional) fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, lin, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

ELSE/autofade2~.body: [autofade2~] is an automatic fade in/out (with separate time for fade in/out) for multichannel inputs. It responds to a gate control and uses internal lookup tables for 7 different fading curves. A gate-on happens when the last input value was zero and the incoming value isn't. A gate-off happens when the last value was a non-0 value and the incoming value is 0 The maximum gain depends on the gate on level.

title: autofade~

messages.description: input signal to be faded in/out
fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>
input signal to be faded in/out
a 1 or 0 is sent when the fade in/out is finished

arguments.description: (optional) fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, lin, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

ELSE/autofade~.body: [autofade~] is an automatic fade in/out for multichannel inputs. It responds to a gate control and uses internal lookup tables for 7 different fading curves. A gate-on happens when the last input value was zero and the incoming value isn't. A gate-off happens when the last value was a non-0 value and the incoming value is 0! The maximum gain depends on the gate on level.

title: autotune
ELSE/autotune.description: retune to a close scale step
ELSE/autotune.body: [autotune] receives a scale as a list of steps in cents and a base MIDI pitch value (default 60). It then retunes incoming MIDI pitches to the closest step in the scale.

title: autotune2
ELSE/autotune2.description: retune to a close scale step

messages.description: pitch value in cents to be retuned

ELSE/autotune2.body: [autotune2] receives a scale as a list of steps in cents and then retunes incoming cents values to the closest step in the scale.

title: avg

messages.description: number to add to the moving average

ELSE/avg.body: [avg] calculates the mean average of all received numbers so far. A clear message resets the object.

title: balance~
ELSE/balance~.body: [balance~] performs an equal power (sin/cos) stereo balancing.

title: bandpass~

arguments.description: resonance, either in Q (default) or bandwidth

flags.name: -bw
flags.description: sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

methods.description: clears filter's memory if you blow it up
sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

ELSE/bandpass~.body: [bandpass~] is a 2nd order bandpass resonant filter. Unlike [else/resonant~], it has a maximum and constant gain at 0dB.

title: bandstop~

flags.name: -bw
flags.description: sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

methods.description: clears filter's memory if you blow it up
sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

ELSE/bandstop~.body: [bandstop~] is a 2nd order band reject filter, it can also be used as a 'notch' filter.

title: bangdiv

messages.description: subsequent bangs until divisor value is hit

methods.description: resets the counter to the start value

ELSE/bangdiv.body: [bangdiv] outputs bangs when receiving bangs by sorting them based on a corresponding divisor value. The first bang in the count is output to the left outlet, while subsequent bangs are sent to the right outlet until the divisor value is hit, starting the cycle again. A starting value lets you start in the middle of a count. If the start value is negative, it adds that many counts to the first time a bang is output.

title: batch.rec~

messages.description: signal inputs to record for each channel
outputs a bang when the object is done recording

methods.description: starts recording for given amount of time in ms. If no float is given, the last set value is used
sets file name (no symbol opens dialog box)

ELSE/batch.rec~.body: [batch.rec~] is a convenient abstraction based on [writesf~] and the 'fast-forward' message to pd that batch records to a sound file that you can load with objects like [sample~], [play.file~], [player~] and others.

title: batch.write~

messages.description: signal inputs to record for each channel
signal inputs to record for each channel
outputs a bang when the object is done recording

ELSE/batch.write~.body: [batch.write~] is a convenient abstraction based on [tabwriter~] and the 'fast-forward' message to pd that batch records to arrays (from an object like [sample~]).

title: beat~

methods.description: set analysis window size in samples

ELSE/beat~.body: [beat~] takes an input signal and outputs a detected bpm value.

title: bend.in

flags.name: -ext
flags.description:
only listen to external input source
default: no

methods.description: non zero sets to listen only to external input source

ELSE/bend.in.body: [bend.in] extracts MIDI Pitch Bend information from raw MIDI input (such as from [midiin]). By default, output values are normalized to floats from -1 to 1, but you can change it to "raw" mode (output integers from 0 to 16383).

title: bend.out
ELSE/bend.out.body: [bend.out] formats and sends raw MIDI pitch bend messages. By default, it takes normalized values (floats from -1 to 1). An argument sets channel number (the default is 1).

title: bicoeff

messages.description: <allpass, lowpass, highpass, bandpass, resonant, bandstop, eq, lowshelf, highshelf>

ELSE/bicoeff.body: [bicoeff] is a GUI that generates coefficients for vanilla's [biquad~] according to different filter types ("eq" filter by default as in the example below). Click on it and drag it around.

title: bicoeff2

messages.description: sets frequency, Q/Slope and gain, and then outputs coefficients
sets filter type <allpass, lowpass, highpass, bandpass, resonant, bandstop, eq, lowshelf, highshelf, off>. It takes 3 more optional arguments that set frequency, Q/Slope and gain

methods.description: sets gain in dB and outputs coefficients

ELSE/bicoeff2.body: [bicoeff2] generates coefficients for vanilla's [biquad~] according to different filter types. All inlets are hot!

title: bin.shift~

messages.description: sets number of bins to shift

ELSE/bin.shift~.body: [bin.shift~] is meant to be used for spectral processing, where you can shift samples (or frequency bins). It is similar to Pd Vanilla's [lrshift~] object, which acts in any block size and shifts samples to the left or right.

title: biplot

messages.description: list of coefficients as taken by [biquad~]
list of coefficients is passed through

ELSE/biplot.body: [biplot] plots the filter response from biquad coefficients, the ones you send to [biquad~] or [biquads~].

title: biquads~

methods.description: <1> bypasses the input, <0> turns the filter on

ELSE/biquads~.body: [biquads~] is a series of biquad filters in cascade. Each biquad section is defined as a list of 5 coefficients (in the same way as Pd Vanilla's [biquad~]). Below we have a series of two biquad sections in cascade. The maximum number of filter stages is 50, which provides up to 100th order filters.

title: bitnormal~
ELSE/bitnormal~.description: replace NaN/Inf and denormal signals with 0

messages.description: input whose NaN/Inf/denormal values are replaced with 0

ELSE/bitnormal~.body: [bitnormal~] replaces NaN (not a number), +/- infinity values and denormals of an incoming signal with zero (hence, it only lets 'normal' floats through. This is useful in conjunction with the bitwise operators in [op~], [expr~] or any other situation where these values are possible.

title: bl.imp2~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
<channel, freq> sets a single frequency channel

ELSE/bl.imp2~.body: [bl.imp2~] is a two sided impulse oscillator like [else/imp2~], but it is bandlimited with the upsampling/filtering technique. This makes the object quite inefficient CPU wise, but is an easy way to implement a bandlimited oscillator.

title: bl.imp~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
<channel, freq> sets a single frequency channel

ELSE/bl.imp~.body: [bl.imp~] is an impulse oscillator like [else/imp~], but it is bandlimited.

title: bl.osc~

messages.description: waveform - saw, saw2, tri, square, imp

arguments.description: (optional) waveform - saw, saw2, tri, square, imp

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/bl.osc~.body: [bl.osc~] is a bandlimited oscillator based on [wavetable~]. It has wavetables built with a variable number of partials for different waveforms: 'saw', 'saw2', 'tri', 'square' and 'imp'.

title: bl.saw2~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/bl.saw2~.body: [bl.saw2~] is a sawtooth oscillator like [else/saw2~], but it is bandlimited.

title: bl.saw~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode
<channel, freq> sets a single frequency channel

ELSE/bl.saw~.body: [bl.saw~] is a sawtooth oscillator like [else/saw~], but it is bandlimited.

title: bl.square~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode
<channel, freq> sets a single frequency channel

ELSE/bl.square~.body: [bl.square~] is a square oscillator like [else/square~], but it is bandlimited.

title: bl.tri~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode
<channel, freq> sets a single frequency channel

ELSE/bl.tri~.body: [bl.tri~] is a triangular oscillator like [else/tri~], but it is bandlimited.

title: bl.vsaw~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode
<channel, freq> sets a single frequency channel

ELSE/bl.vsaw~.body: [bl.vsaw~] is a variable sawtooth waveform oscillator that also becomes a triangular osccilator just like [else/vsaw~], but it is bandlimited.

title: bl.wavetable~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

methods.description: sets an entire array to be used as a waveform
sets size in number of points
non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/bl.wavetable~.body: [bl.wavetable~] is a wavetable oscillator like [else/wavetable~], but it is bandlimited with the upsampling/filtering technique. This makes the object quite inefficient CPU wise, but is an easy way to implement a bandlimited oscillator. This object also minimizes aliasing caused by hard sync and phase modulation.

title: blip~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/blip~.body: [blip~] uses DSF (Discrete-Summation Formulae) to generate waveforms as a sum of cosines. It takes a frequency in Hz for the fundamental, a number of harmonics, a multiplier for the partials after the first one and the lowest harmonic number (by default it generates an impulse waveform). This object is based on Csound's 'gbuzz' opcode.

title: blocksize~

messages.description: get block size period or frequency

methods.description: set and get the block size period in samples
set and get the sample rate period in ms
set and get the block size frequency in Hz

ELSE/blocksize~.body: [blocksize~] reports the block size when the audio is turned on, when it changes or via a bang. The period is reported in samples or ms, but you can also query the corresponding frequency in Hz. The default output is the block size in samples. The DSP needs to be turned on so a block size is detected!

title: bpbank~

messages.description: signal to be filtered via a bank of bandpass filters
the signal filtered by the bank of filters

flags.name: -freq <list>
flags.description: sets list of frequencies for all filters

flags.name: -amp <list>
flags.description: sets list of amplitudes for all filters

flags.name: -q <list>
flags.description: sets list of resonance (Q) for all filters

flags.name: -ramp <list>
flags.description: sets list of ramp time for all filters

flags.name: -rampall <float>
flags.description: sets ramp time for all filters

methods.description: list of frequencies for all filters in the bank
list of resonance (Q) for all filters in the bank
list of amplitudes for all filters in the bank
list of ramp time for all filters in the bank
ramp time for all filters in the bank

ELSE/bpbank~.body: [bpbank~] is a bank made of [bandpass~] objects. You can set any number of filters with the first argument and control the parameters for each filter. If you use flags, the number of elements in the list (such as the frequency list) sets the number of filters in the bank (you shouldn't use arguments if you use flags).

title: bpm
ELSE/bpm.body: [bpm] calculates a conversion to and from bpm (beats per minute). By default it converts to and from ms, but you can also set it to convert to and from samples.

title: break
ELSE/break.body: [break] can break a list with a given symbol separator set via the argument.

title: brickwall~
ELSE/brickwall~.body: [brickwall~] a 10th order butterworth lowpass filter, which is filter with a steep cutoff slope (60 dB by octave). By default, its cutoff frequency is 0.75 * Nyquist, but you can change it with the 1st argument or the second inlet.

title: brown

messages.description: random value based on brownian motion

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/brown.body: [brown] is a control brownian motion generator. It is a bounded random walk (based on a pseudo random number generator algorithm). By default, the maximum step is "0.125", which is a ratio of the whole range (and the output range is from 0 to 127). If it tries to step outside the bounds, it just gets folded back.

title: brown~

messages.description: impulses get a new random step

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/brown~.body: [brown~] is a brown noise generator (aka brownian noise or red noise), whose spectral energy drops 6dB per octave (sounding less hissy than white noise). It is implemented as a bounded random walk (based on a pseudo random number generator algorithm). By default, the maximum step is 0.125, but you can set that. If a signal is connected, non-0 values generate new random steps.

title: buffer

messages.description: resize, set array and output list

methods.description: 1st item is index, the next are values from that index

ELSE/buffer.body: [buffer] does not much and nothing that [array] can't, but it's a convenient object for getting and setting array values, kinda like a storage object such as [message]. You can get/set arrays from actual arrays, [table] or [array] objects.

title: button

messages.description: sets horizontal and vertical size in pixels
sets both horizontal and vertical size in pixels

flags.name: size <float>
flags.description: sets both horizontal and vertical size in pixels

methods.description: sets horizontal and vertical size in pixels
sets both horizontal and vertical size in pixels

ELSE/button.body: [button] is a GUI button that responds to mouse clicks. When clicked on, it sends a "1" value, and outputs "0" when releasing the mouse button.

title: canvas.active
ELSE/canvas.active.body: The [canvas.active] object outputs activity status. It sends 1 if the window is active (when it's the front-most window) and 0 when inactive. It can also query the parent status with the depth argument (1 goes up to the parent patch, 2 queries the parent's parent and so on).

title: canvas.bounds
ELSE/canvas.bounds.body: The [canvas.bounds] object outputs bounds coordinates in pixels, top left coordinate (horizontal, vertical) and bottom right (horizontal, vertical). Try moving the patch window or change window size to see changes.

title: canvas.edit
ELSE/canvas.edit.body: The [canvas.edit] object outputs edit status - 1 when the window is in edit mode and 0 when it is in run mode.

title: canvas.gop

messages.description: GOP info <gop status, x size, y size, coords (x1, y1, x2, y1)>

ELSE/canvas.gop.body: The [canvas.gop] object outputs the information of a "graph on parent" (aka 'GOP'), which is a subpatch with a visible graph in the parent. It can also query the GOP info with the depth argument (1 goes up to the parent patch, 2 queries the parent's parent and so on).

title: canvas.mouse

arguments.description: non-0 allows output in edit mode

ELSE/canvas.mouse.body: [canvas.mouse] gets mouse click and mouse coordinates when your mouse is interacting with the canvas window. An optional argument sets the depth level. This object doesn't output anything if in edit mode.

title: canvas.name
ELSE/canvas.name.body: [canvas.name] gives you the canvas/window name symbol.

title: canvas.pos
ELSE/canvas.pos.body: The [canvas.pos] object outputs the position of a subwindow canvas in the parent canvas. The output is the x/y coordinates. It can also query the parent status with the depth argument (1 goes up to the parent patch, 2 queries the parent's parent and so on).

title: canvas.setname

methods.description: non zero sets to abstraction mode

ELSE/canvas.setname.body: [canvas.setname] sets a symbol name to a canvas so you can send it messages. It's similar to [namecanvas] but it also allows you to set the name of a parent patch with the second optional depth argument - (1) is parent patch (2) is parent's parent patch and so on...

title: canvas.vis
ELSE/canvas.vis.body: The [canvas.vis] object outputs visibility status. It sends 1 if the window is visible and 0 when it's not. It can also query the parent status with the depth argument (1 goes up to the parent patch, 2 queries the parent's parent and so on). The object can react to most visibility changes and you can also query for it with a bang.

title: canvas.zoom
ELSE/canvas.zoom.body: The [canvas.zoom] object outputs zoom status at load time and when it changes - it outputs 1 when the window is in zoom mode and 0 when it is not. You can also query with a bang.

title: car2pol

messages.description: converts the last received coordinates pair
real part from the Cartesian coordinates
imaginary part from the Cartesian coordinates
phase in radians (-pi to pi) of the polar form

ELSE/car2pol.body: [car2pol] converts Cartesian coordinates (real / imaginary) to polar coordinates (amplitude / phase).

title: car2pol~

messages.description: real part from the Cartesian coordinates
imaginary part form the Cartesian coordinates
phase in radians (-pi to pi) of the polar form

ELSE/car2pol~.body: [car2pol~] converts Cartesian coordinates (real / imaginary) to polar coordinates (amplitude / phase).

title: ceil~
ELSE/ceil~.body: [ceil~] is a ceiling math function for signals.

title: cents2frac
ELSE/cents2frac.body: Use [cents2frac] to convert a list of cents to intervals defined as fraction symbols or lists.

title: cents2ratio

messages.description: convert or output the last converted value (only float)

methods.description: sets next value to be converted via bang (only float)

ELSE/cents2ratio.body:
Use [cents2ratio] to convert intervals in cents to interval as rational numbers (expressed as floating point decimals). The conversion formula is;
rational = pow(2, (cents/1200))

title: cents2ratio~
ELSE/cents2ratio~.body:
Use [cents2ratio~] to convert a signal representing an interval in cents to an interval represented as a decimal rational number. The conversion formula is;
ratio = pow(2, (cents/1200))

title: cents2scale
ELSE/cents2scale.body: Use [cents2scale] to convert a list of intervals defined as cents to a scale defined as scale steps in semitones.

title: chance

messages.description: a bang to be passed or not
updates arguments if more than 1 is given
sets chance number if only one argument

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/chance.body: When [chance] receives a bang, it outputs to an outlet according to its chance (probability weight). The total number is the sum of all arguments. It also has an index mode where it outputs the outlet index number instead.

title: chance~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/chance~.body: When [chance~] receives an impulse, it outputs it to an outlet according to its chance (probability weight). The total number of chances is the sum of all arguments.

title: changed

messages.description: force the last data to be output
sets a current message inside the object
any message that's different from the previous

ELSE/changed.body: [changed] passes its data input through only when it changed from the last receive message or the message that was set via arguments or messages. Unlike [change] from Pd Vanilla, it accepts any kind of messages.

title: changed2~
ELSE/changed2~.body: [changed2~] sends an impulse whenever an input value changes its direction. The right outlet sends the status information (1 when increasing, -1 when decreasing and 0 for no change).

title: changed~

messages.description: impulse when a change is detected

ELSE/changed~.body: [changed~] sends an impulse whenever an input value changes and the change is equal or greater than a given threshold. If the threshold is 0, then any change is detected.

title: chorus~
ELSE/chorus~.body: [chorus~] is a simple mono chorus effect abstraction. It uses an internal comb filter and has only one extra voice.

title: chrono

messages.description: rewinds chronometer or non-0 starts or continues, zero stops
sets timer length in minutes / seconds

flags.name: -h
flags.description: also outputs hours for the chronometer

ELSE/chrono.body: [chrono] is a stopwatch chronometer or a timer.

title: circle

messages.description: sets x, y and outputs it

flags.name: -fgcolor <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets foreground color in RGB
default: 0 0 0

flags.name: -savestate <float>
flags.description:
non-0 sets will save object state
default: 0

flags.name: -range <f, f>
flags.description:
sets range (minimum and maximum values of x and y)
default: -1 1

flags.name: -bgcolor1 <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets in bounds back color in RGB
default: 255 255 255

flags.name: -bgcolor2 <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets out of bounds color in RGB
default: 255 255 255

flags.name: -bgcolor <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets both background colors in RGB
default: 255 255 255

flags.name: -clip <float>
flags.description:
non-0 sets to clip mode
default: 1

methods.description: sets range (minimum and maximum values of x and y)
sets range (minimum and maximum values of y)
sets range (minimum and maximum values of x)
sets background color inside circle in RGB
sets background color outside circle in RGB
sets both background colors in RGB

ELSE/circle.body: [circle] is a two dimensional slider GUI abstraction (see also [else/slider2d).

title: circuit~

methods.description: appends a message to the note
appends a message to the note
appends a message to the note

ELSE/circuit~.body: [circuit~] simulates analog circuitry from a text description of the circuit separated by semicolons. Voltage, current, resistance and potmeter can be modulated by an input signal by passing '$sn' as the first (where 'n' is the inlet number indexed from 1). To create an audio input, use a voltage source with a modulatable parameter. An audio outlet will be created for every probe component. The object can be quite CPU intensive!

title: click

messages.description: bang when clicked on in the parent patch

arguments.description: sets mode, 0 is "abstraction" mode, non-0 is "subpatch" mode

ELSE/click.body:
[click] sends a bang when you put it in an abstraction or a subpatch and click on the parent. This is useful for making non graphical abstractions respond to a click in the same way compiled objects can. 
See below how it sends a bang when you click on the subpatch. Note that you can still open the subpatch with right click and then "open".

title: clock

messages.description: non-0 starts the clock, zero stops it
BPM for main clock or multiplier for synced clock

arguments.description: BPM for main clock, or divider for synced clocks
arguments.default: 120 for main clock, 1 for synced clocks

flags.name: -s
flags.description:
set to synced clock
default: main

ELSE/clock.body:
A [clock] object sends bangs at a regular tempo and controls other synced [clock] objects with a relative tempo.
By default, [clock] works as a main clock and you can set it to 'synced' with the '-s' flag. A synced clock is controlled by the main clock, so it only works when the main clock is on. A synced clock also has a relative tempo in relation to the main clock tempo (as in a clock multiplier) and can be synced to a [metronome].
You should have only one main clock, but you can have several clocks synced to this main one. By default, clock gets local names according to the patch window, but you can set a different name so you can have different clocks named differently.
Below we have a main clock at 60 BPM and a synced clock that divider the tempo to a factor of two (so it plays twice as fast).

title: coeff2pz

messages.description: overall gain of the filter response

ELSE/coeff2pz.body: [coeff2pz] converts an input of biquad coefficients to of 2 poles/zeros coordinates.

title: colors

messages.description: color values (in RGB, hex, iemgui or ds)

flags.name: -rgb
flags.description:
sets to RGB list output
default: hexadecimal

flags.name: -iemgui
flags.description:
sets to iemguis output
default: hexadecimal

flags.name: -ds
flags.description:
sets to Data Structures output
default: hexadecimal

methods.description: CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, and Key) color values
HSL (Hue, Saturation, and Lightness) color values
HSV (Hue, Saturation, and Value) color values

ELSE/colors.body: [colors] is a color picker and converter. You can set the output format and also convert from other input formats. Colors that you pick and colors you send to the object are stored and recalled next time you open the color picker. This object is useful for setting color properties inside abstractions.

title: comb.filt~

arguments.description: maximum and initial delay time in ms

methods.description: non-0 sets to gain mode instead of 't60'

ELSE/comb.filt~.body: Use [comb.filt~] as a resonator comb filter.

title: comb.rev~

arguments.description: maximum and initial delay time in ms

methods.description: changes the maximum delay size (in ms)

ELSE/comb.rev~.body: Use [comb.rev~] as a reverberator, comb filter and delay.

title: combine

messages.description: message to be combined with another input close in time
time interval for combining items to a list

ELSE/combine.body: [combine] collects messages (with one or more elements) into a single list if they come within a certain given amount of time, but not if greater than it. A "0" time is possible if messages are sent in a sequence such as from a [loop] object. Each item or list is appended to the previous stored items. A negative time value will not combine anything.

title: compress~

methods.description: RMS average size in samples (from 1 to 512)

ELSE/compress~.body: [compress~] is an abstraction that performs compression. It attenuates an input signal above a given threshold.

title: conv~
ELSE/conv~.body: [conv~] is an abstraction that performs partitioned convolution. It takes a partition size and Impulse Response sound file.

title: cosine~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/cosine~.body: [cosine~] is a sinusoidal oscillator that accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for phase sync and phase modulation.

title: count

messages.description: sets new count value and outputs it

arguments.description: 1 float sets max / 2 floats set min & max

methods.description: without a float, resets to the reset point, but a float sets a new reset point as well
non-0 sets to alternating mode (between up/down)

ELSE/count.body: [count] counts and wraps between a minimum and maximum value. It has modes for counting up, down and alternating between them.

title: crackle~
crossover~
ELSE/crossover~.body:
[crossover~] is a of 3rd order butterworth crossover filter.
It has two outlets for lowpass (left) and highpass (right) filters that you can use separately or in combination to form a crossover filter.

title: crusher~

messages.description: signal to be crushed (resampled and quantized)

ELSE/crusher~.body: [crusher~] is a bit-crusher/reducer and decimator abstraction based on the objects [quantizer~] and [downsample~], where an input signal is resampled and quantized.

title: ctl.in

arguments.description: one value sets channel number. Two values set control number

ELSE/ctl.in.body: [ctl.in] extracts MIDI control information from raw MIDI input (such as from [midiin]).

title: ctl.out

arguments.description: one float sets channel number. two floats set control number and channel

ELSE/ctl.out.body: [ctl.out] formats and sends raw MIDI control messages. An argument sets channel number (the default is 1).

title: cusp~

messages.description: <channel, freq> set a single frequency channel

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

ELSE/cusp~.body:
[cusp~] is a chaotic generator using the difference equation;
y[n] = a - b * sqrt(abs(y[n-1]))
The output rate of the equation is given in Hz (default: Nyquist).
Object based on SuperCollider's "CuspN" UGEN.

title: damp.osc~

messages.description: frequency and trigger value (normalized to MIDI range)
damped oscillator with cosine initial phase
damped oscillator with sine initial phase

flags.name: -midi
flags.description: sets to MIDI pitch frequency input

methods.description: sets to MIDI pitch frequency input

ELSE/damp.osc~.body: [damp.osc~] is based on a complex pole resonant filter ([vcf2~]) with two ouputs (real/cosine and imaginary/sine). It takes frequency in hertz or midi and a decay time in ms. It is triggered by signals at zero to non zero transitions or by lists at control rate.

title: datetime

messages.description: time: hour, min, sec at bang or seed value via seed message

methods.description: get number of seconds since the beginning of the month to use it as a seed value

ELSE/datetime.body: [datetime] gives us current/local date (right outlet) and time (left outlet) as lists.

title: daw_storage
ELSE/daw_storage.description: store data inside DAW session state

arguments.description: unique ID for DAW storage data

ELSE/daw_storage.body: [daw_storage] allows storage of arrays, lists, symbols, as extradata lists in the DAW session.

title: db2lin

arguments.description: minimum value (can be as low as "-Inf")

ELSE/db2lin.body: [db2lin] converts amplitude values from deciBel Full Scale (dBFS) to linear. By default, a minimum value of -100 becomes zero - in theory, zero is minus infinity and an argument sets another minimum dB value to correspond to minus infinity. Conversion expression is: amp = pow(10, dBFS / 20).

title: db2lin~

arguments.description: minimum value (can be as low as "-Inf")

ELSE/db2lin~.body: [db2lin~] converts amplitude values from deciBel Full Scale (dBFS) to linear. By default, a minimum value of -100 becomes zero - in theory, zero is minus infinity and an argument sets another minimum dB value to correspond to minus infinity. Conversion expression is: amp = pow(10, dBFS / 20).

title: dbgain~
ELSE/dbgain~.description: adjust gain to a dB level
ELSE/dbgain~.body: The [dbgain~] object is basically like [level~], but without the GUI. One difference is that it allows multichannels on the right inlet to set different dB levels.

title: dec2frac

flags.name: -list
flags.description: sets to list mode (output fraction as a list)

ELSE/dec2frac.body: Use [dec2frac] to convert a list of decimals to intervals defined as fraction symbols or lists.

title: dec2hex

messages.description: decimal value(s) to convert (outputs the result)

ELSE/dec2hex.body: [dec2hex] converts decimal values to hexadecimal ones. The output is preceded by "0x" and the letters in the hexadecimal values are upper case. You can also convert lists. Only floats are converted, symbols are ignored.

title: decay~
ELSE/decay~.body: [decay~] is based on SuperCollider's "Decay" UGEN. It is a one pole filter that creates an exponential decay from impulses. The decay time (in ms) is how long it takes for the signal to decay 60dB. The same filter is also used in other objects from the ELSE library (asr~/adsr~/lag~/lag2~).

title: default

messages.description: outputs the message without storing it

ELSE/default.body: The [default] sets a default message as the argument or via the right inlet. A bang outputs the default message, other messages are sent through without being stored.

title: deg2rad

messages.description: input degree value to convert to radians

flags.name: -pos
flags.description:
wrap to positive only
default: allow also negative

ELSE/deg2rad.body: [deg2rad] converts degrees (0 to 360) to radians (0 to 2*pi). You can also convert negative values from 0 to -360 (0 to -2*pi), unless you give it the -pos, so values are wrapped to positive. Values outside the input range are wrapped.

title: delace
ELSE/delace.body: [delace] deinterleaves a list according to the number of outlets.

title: delace~
ELSE/delace~.body: [delace~] deinterleaves a multichannel signal according to the number of outlets.

title: delete

messages.description: remaining elements from the input message
deleted elements or bang when deleting fails

ELSE/delete.body: [delete] deletes one or more elements from an input message. The mid inlet or 1st argument sets the element index and the right inlet or 2nd argument sets number of elements to delete. Right outlet outputs deleted item(s) and left outlet the remaining elements from the list. Right outlet sends a bang if deleting fails (if index and/or n elements are out of range). An index of 0 means no items are deleted!. Negative indexes count backwards (-1 is the last element and so on).

title: del~ in

messages.description: signal input into the delay line
dummy outlet to force order of execution

arguments.description: delay size in ms or samples

flags.name: -samps
flags.description:
sets time value to samples
default: ms

ELSE/del~ in.body: Define a delay line and write to it. If neither "in" or "out" is specified as the first argument, the default it [del~ in].

title: del~ out

messages.description: signal input into the delay line

arguments.description: delay size in ms or samples

flags.name: -samps
flags.description:
sets time value to samples
default: ms

ELSE/del~ out.body: Read from a delay line. It uses a cubic (4 point) interpolation called spline and considers the buffer to be circular (so index 0 to table size is accepted).

title: del~
ELSE/del~.body: [del~] sets and writes to a delay line if created as [del~ in] (default) and reads from it (with interpolation) if created as [del~ out]. It's quite similar to [delread~]/[delread4~], but with more features.

title: detect~

messages.description: any signal to trigger the object

ELSE/detect~.body: [detect~] detects the frequency/period between trigger events. A trigger happens on a transition from non positive to positive.

messages.description: 1 if opened a new directory, 0 if it couldn't open

arguments.description: directory level, 0 — current patch's directory, 1 — parent's patch's, etc

methods.description: opens a directory and loads contents, no symbol opens binary's directory
reloads contents of the last opened directory
outputs the current directory in the right outlet
dump files from directory in the (second outlet)
output number of found files in the (third outlet)
seeks and output the file corresponding to that number
increments seek value and outputs file

title: dispatch
ELSE/dispatch.description: dispatch elements of a list to different locations
ELSE/dispatch.body: [dispatch] gets a list and sends each element to a given address (order is left to right).

title: display
ELSE/display.body: [display] can display a message just like [print]. The 1st argument specifies a fixed number of characters to display. If "0" or no argument is given, the display automatically resizes according to the input message. If a message is larger than the fixed number of characters, the displayed message is truncated.

title: dollsym

messages.description: a symbol to be expanded if it contains dollar args
a one element symbol is also expanded

ELSE/dollsym.body: [dollsym] gets and expands the value of dollar symbols ("$0-x", "$1-y", and so on). It can also get and expand according to the values of a parent patch with the 'depth' argument. Symbols without dollar arguments are passed through unchanged.

title: downsample~

messages.description: rate (in Hz) used to downsample the input signal

ELSE/downsample~.body: [downsample~] samples a signal received in the left inlet at a frequency rate in Hz. It can operate with or without interpolation.

title: drive~
ELSE/drive~.body: [drive~] simulates an analog "soft clipping" distortion by applying non-linear transfer functions.

title: drum.seq

messages.description: gets sequence value and moves to the next
sets track, slot and status (no output)
sets slot value and output tracks values
when reaching the end of the sequence

flags.name: -size <float>
flags.description:
sets cell size in pixels
default: 20

flags.name: -embed
flags.description:
sets embedding mode
default: no embedding

methods.description: sets track values (1st value sets track number)
export tracks, no float exports all as an array
sets cell size in pixels (clears data)
non-0 save internal contents with the object
array of contexts via the "export" message

ELSE/drum.seq.body: [drum.seq] provides a drum grid so you can activate cells to represent attacks.

title: drunkard

messages.description: sets new current value and outputs it
random number output as result of the random walk

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal
sets probability of a positive step in %

ELSE/drunkard.body: [drunkard] generates random numbers within a given step range from the last output. When reaching the bounds, the values get wrapped.

title: drunkard~

messages.description: two floats set lower and upper bound
random value as result of the random walk

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal
sets probability of a positive step in %

ELSE/drunkard~.body: [drunkard~] generates random values within a given step range from the last output. When reaching the bounds, the values get wrapped.

title: duck~

messages.description: ducked signal alone or mixed with control signal

ELSE/duck~.body: [duck~] is an abstraction that performs a duck volume effect (a.k.a sidechain compression). It attenuates an input signal according to the level of a control signal. you can also mix the ducked signal with the control input signal.

title: dust2~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/dust2~.body: [dust2~] is based on SuperCollider's "Dust2" UGEN and outputs random impulse values (from -1 to 1) at random times according to a density parameter. The difference to SuperCollider's is that it only produces actual impulses (one non-0 value in between 0 valued samples).

title: dust~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/dust~.body: [dust~] is based on SuperCollider's "Dust" UGEN and outputs random impulse values (only positive values up to 1) at random times according to a density parameter. The difference to SuperCollider's is that it only produces actual impulses (one non-0 value in between 0 valued samples).

title: e

messages.description: calculate or output the last calculated value
the value of e times the multiplier

ELSE/e.body: [e] calculates and outputs the value of 'e'. It receives a multiplier value via the argument or second inlet, which needs to be greater than 0 (otherwise it's considered as 1).

title: echo.rev~
ELSE/echo.rev~.body: [echo.rev~] is an echo/reverb abstraction. It only contains feedforward lines and can be used to produce early reflections in a reverb algorithm. But it can also be used on its own.

title: else

messages.description: ELSE version (major, minor, bugfix, status, status number)

methods.description: prints library information on the terminal
outputs version information as a list

ELSE/else.body: The else binary does gives some basic information about the ELSE library in Pd's window at creation time (but only once if multiple else objects are loaded) and loads an object browser plugin. It accepts the "about" message that prints this basic information (version, release date, etc) on the terminal on demand and also accepts the "version" message that outputs the version information as a list.

title: envelope~

arguments.description: sin, hann, tri, vsaw <float>, gauss <float>, trapezoid <float, float>

methods.description: set envelope type and arguments: <sin>, <hann>, <tri>, <vsaw, float>, <gauss float>, and <trap float float>

ELSE/envelope~.body: [envelope~] provides 6 different envelopes, which are generated via a phase input.

title: envgen~

messages.description: attacks the envelope with the last gate on value
sets and runs envelope (pairs of duration & target. if odd, 1st float is starting point)

arguments.description: <f> — initial output value, <f,f,..> sets the envelope

flags.name: -exp <list>
flags.description: sets function with an extra exponential element for each segment

flags.name: -legato
flags.description: sets to legato mode on, default is off

flags.name: curve <float/symbol>
flags.description: sets curve for all segments (default linear)

methods.description: sets exponential values for each line segment
same as bang or gate on
resumes the envelope after being paused
non-0 sets to samples instead of ms

ELSE/envgen~.body: [envgen~] is an envelope (and an all purpose line) generator (here it creates a 1000 ms line to 1 and 500 ms line to 0).

title: eqdiv

messages.description: set number of divisions and generate scale

flags.name: -ratio
flags.description: sets scale output to ratio instead of cents

ELSE/eqdiv.body: Use [eqdiv] to generate a scale as a list of ratios that equally divide a given interval, the output is in cents. intervals defined as fractions to ratio intervals.

title: equal
ELSE/equal.body: [equal] compares lists and outputs 1 if they're the same or 0 if they're not. The arguments set the right input.

title: eq~

flags.name: -bw
flags.description: sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

methods.description: clears filter's memory if you blow it up
sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

ELSE/eq~.body: [eq~] is a 2nd order parametric equalizer filter, it can be used as a peak and a notch filter.

title: euclid
ELSE/euclid.body: [euclid] implements an Euclidean rhythm algorithm. It takes a step number and returns if that step is a hit or a rest depending on parameters of: number of steps, number of hits and rotation value.

title: expand~
ELSE/expand~.body: [expand~] is an abstraction that performs expanding. It attenuates an input signal below a given threshold.

title: factor
ELSE/factor.body: [factor] is a factorization function. It receives a float and spits a list of its factors.

title: fader~

arguments.description: fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

methods.description: fade types <quartic, sin, sqrt, hann, lin, hannsin, linsin>

ELSE/fader~.body: [fader~] is a waveshaper. It takes input from 0 to 1 and uses internal lookup tables for 7 different fading curves.

title: fbdelay~

messages.description: signal input to the delay line

flags.name: -size <float>
flags.description:
sets delay size
default: 1000 ms or first argument's value if given

flags.name: -samps
flags.description:
sets delay time unit to "samples"
default: ms

methods.description: changes the maximum delay size (in ms)

ELSE/fbdelay~.body: Use [fbdelay~] for delay effects, reverberation and comb filtering. By default, you can set a delay time and a reverberation/decay time in ms ("t60"), which is the time the impulse takes to fall 60dB in energy (but you can change this parameter to a gain coefficient).

title: fbsine2~
ELSE/fbsine2~.description: feedback sine with chaotic phase indexing

messages.description: 2 floats set x[n-1] and y[n-1] respectively

arguments.description: sets initial phase value of y[n]

methods.description: clears values of x[n-1] and y[n-1]

title: fbsine~

methods.description: turns mean filter on <1> or off <0>

ELSE/fbsine~.body: [fbsine~] is a sinusoidal oscillator with phase modulation feedback. Like [sine~], it accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for phase modulation and phase sync. Additionally, it has a feedback value.

title: fdn.rev~

messages.description: left output of the FDN reverberator
right output of the FDN reverberator

flags.name: -time <float>
flags.description:
t60 reverberation time in seconds
default: 4

methods.description: set number of lines and min/max times
clears the delay lines and reverberation
print delay lines and other parameters in Pd's window

ELSE/fdn.rev~.body: [fdn.rev~] is a feedback delay network reverberator which can be used for late reflections (a.k.a reverb tail). The main parameters are: decay time (t60) and high frequency damping.

title: ffdelay~

messages.description: signal input into the delay line

flags.name: -size <float>
flags.description:
delay size, defines the delay's maximum time
default: 1000 ms or argument's value

flags.name: -samps
flags.description:
sets delay time unit to "samples"
default: ms

ELSE/ffdelay~.body: [ffdelay~] is simple feed forward delay with interpolation.

title: filterdelay~
ELSE/filterdelay~.body: [filterdelay~] is a high level delay unit that goes thgouh a resonant lowpass filter, a soft clipper and a DC filter. The processed delay output is also fed back into the delay line.

title: flanger~

flags.name: -tri
flags.description:
sets waveform to triangular
default: sine

ELSE/flanger~.body: [flanger~] is a simple flanger FX object.

title: float2bits

messages.description: sets float value and converts to bits
sets float to convert to bits
a list of bits when receiving a bang or "signal", "exponent" and "mantissa" values when receiving a 'split' message

arguments.description: sets float to convert to bits

methods.description: sets float value and doesn't convert it

ELSE/float2bits.body: [float2bits] converts decimal float (IEEE754 Single precision 32-bit) values to binary form (a list of 32 bits). By the way, this is the type of float used in Pure Data.

title: float2imp~

messages.description: set impulse value and generate impulse

ELSE/float2imp~.body: [float2imp~] converts floats to impulses with sample accuracy. It is an abstraction based on [vline~], so it can convert to an impulse within an audio block. A bang can be used to output the impulse with the stored value. The default value is '1' and can be set via the argument. The right inlet also sets the impulse value. A float input sets the value and gets converted to an impulse.

title: float2sig~
f2s~

messages.description: float value to convert to signal

ELSE/float2sig~.body: [float2sig~] (or [f2s~] for short) converts floats to signals. The conversion is smoothened by a ramp time in ms.

title: floor~
ELSE/floor~.body: [floor~] is a floor math function for signals.

title: fm~

messages.description: modulation frequency as ratio of carrier

arguments.description: modulation frequency as ratio of carrier

ELSE/fm~.body: [fm~] is a simple frequency modulation unit consisting of a pair of sinusoidal oscillators, where one modulates the frequency input of another. The modulation frequency is set as a ratio of the carrier and the modulation index is multiplied by this resulting frequency. The object has support for multichannels.

title: fold

messages.description: fold or output the last folded value (only float)

arguments.description: 2 floats set min and max, 1 float sets max value (min to 0)

methods.description: sets next value to be folded via bang (only float)

ELSE/fold.body: [fold] folds between a low and high value. This is like a mirroring function, where an out of bounds value folds back until it is in the given range.

title: fold~

arguments.description: 2 floats set min and max, 1 float sets max value (min to 0)

ELSE/fold~.body: [fold~] folds between a low and high value. This is like a mirroring function, where an out of bounds value folds back until it is in the given range.

title: fontsize
ELSE/fontsize.body: The [fontsize] object outputs the patch's font size when the object is loaded and when it changes via the font menu. You can also query with a bang message.

title: format

messages.description: float/symbol atoms to format variables (messages with more than one item and sends the remaining items to the next inlets)

ELSE/format.body: [format] formats messages similarly to [makefilename], but it accepts more than one variable where each corresponds to an inlet. It also allows you to generate messages with more than on element.

title: frac.add

messages.description: fractional value to add to right input
fractional value to add to left input

ELSE/frac.add.body: Use [frac.add] to add two fractions. The output is also a fraction.

title: frac.mul

messages.description: fractional value to multiply to right input
fractional value to multiply to left input

ELSE/frac.mul.body: Use [frac.mul] to multiply two fractions. The output is also a fraction.

title: frac2cents
ELSE/frac2cents.body: Use [frac2cents] to convert a list of intervals defined as fraction symbols to cents.

title: frac2dec
ELSE/frac2dec.body: Use [frac2dec] to convert a single element or list of intervals defined as fraction symbols to decimal floating point numbers. Numbers don't need to be integers and you can also have a second fraction between parenthesis as the denominator or numerator.

title: free.rev~
ELSE/free.rev~.body: [free.rev~] is a stereo reverb abstraction based on the widely known 'freeverb' algorithm.

title: freeze~
ELSE/freeze~.body: [freeze~] is an abstraction based on [sigmund~] (analysis & resynthesis). It contains a bank with oscillators.

title: freq.shift~

messages.description: signal to be shifted in frequency
signal shifted in the opposite direction

arguments.description: the frequency shift value in Hz

ELSE/freq.shift~.body: [freq.shift~] has two outputs, the left outlet is the frequency shifted signal and the right outlet is the signal shifted in the opposite direction.

title: freq2midi

messages.description: set a new value for A4

ELSE/freq2midi.body: [freq2midi] converts frequency to MIDI like [ftom], but it also converts lists and allows you so set a different frequency reference value for A4 so you can explore different tunings.

title: function

messages.description: values from 0-1 are treated as indexes

methods.description: indexes of table (function value is output)
sets new graph bounds according to min/max

ELSE/function.body: [function] is a breakpoints function GUI, mainly used with [function~] or [envgen~]. You can click and drag on it or send it lists.

title: function~

arguments.description: 3 or more floats to set the function

flags.name: -curve <float/symbol>
flags.description: sets curve type for all segments

methods.description: sets exponential values for each line segment

ELSE/function~.body: [function~] generates functions from arguments/list input. Input values from 0 to 1 reads the function. It needs an odd number of elements in a list, staring with 3 values, the syntax is (point1, period, point2, period, point3, etc...). The overall sum of periods is normalized to 1!

title: gain2~

right channel signal whose gain will be adjusted
the adjusted gain signal of the left channel
the adjusted gain signal of the right channel
slider value, from 0-1 (independent of maximum gain)

sets max gain (needs to be > 0)
default: 1

flags.name: -mode <float>
flags.description:
sets scaling mode; 0 (quartic), 1 (dB) or 2 (linear)
default: quartic

methods.description: sets ramp time in ms for slider values (default 20)

ELSE/gain2~.body: [gain2~] is a convenient stereo gain abstraction, so you can adjust a stereo signal's gain. It has controls for ramp time, maximum gain, scaling mode and init.

title: gain~

messages.description: incoming signal whose gain will be adjusted
slider value, from 0-1 (independent of maximum gain)

flags.name: -max <float>
flags.description: sets max gain (needs to be > 0, default 1)

flags.name: -mode <float>
flags.description:
sets scaling mode; 0 (quartic), 1 (dB) or 2 (linear)
default: quartic

methods.description: sets ramp time in ms for slider values (default 20)

ELSE/gain~.body: [gain~] is a convenient mono gain abstraction for adjusting a signal's gain. It has controls for ramp time, maximum gain, scaling mode and init.

title: gate2imp~
ELSE/gate2imp~.body: [gate2imp~] converts gates to impulses. It sends an impulse when receiving a gate (0 to non-0 transitions) where the impulse value is the same as the gate.

title: gatedelay
ELSE/gatedelay.body: [gatedelay] delays a control gate on value, but the gate off is immediately output.

title: gatedelay~
ELSE/gatedelay~.body: [gatedelay~] delays a gate on value, but the gate off is immediately output. The object has support for multichannels

title: gatehold
ELSE/gatehold.body: [gatehold] holds a gate value for a certain amount of time (specified in ms) after the gate has closed. If a new gate starts before the hold time is finished, the object is retriggered.

title: gatehold~
ELSE/gatehold~.body: [gatehold~] holds a gate value for a certain amount of time (specified in ms) after the gate has closed. If a new gate starts before the hold time is finished, the object is retriggered.

title: gaterelease
ELSE/gaterelease.body: [gaterelease] allows you to release one gate in your patch while you still hold another. [gaterelease] releases a gate value after a given amount of time (specified in ms) after the gate has opened. If the gate is closed before the release time, the object is reset. If the release time is 0 or less, the object is bypassed.

title: gaterelease~
ELSE/gaterelease~.body: [gaterelease~] allows you to release one gate in your patch while you still hold another. [gaterelease~] releases a gate value after a given amount of time (specified in ms) after the gate has opened. If the gate is closed before the release time, the object is reset. If the release time is 0 or less, the object is bypassed.

title: gaussian~

flags.name: -midi

flags.name: -mc <list>

methods.description: nonzero sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
<channel, freq> set a single frequency channel
nonzero sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/gaussian~.body: [gaussian~] is a Gaussian oscillator. It accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for width, phase sync and phase modulation.

title: gbman~

messages.description: <channel, freq> set a single frequency channel

arguments.description: sets initial feedback value of y[n-1]

flags.name: -mc <list>

ELSE/gbman~.body: [gbman~] is a gingerbread man map chaotic generator, the output is from the difference equation => y[n] = 1 + abs(y[n-1]) - y[n-2].

title: gcd
ELSE/gcd.body: [gcd] returns the greatest common divisor of two or more numbers. Floats are truncated to integers. Negative values are accepted, "0" is treated as "1". It has two inlets for setting two floats, but a list input to the left sets 2 or more floats to get their gcd.

title: gendyn~

methods.description: sets seed, no float sets unique internal (default)
sets distributions and parameters, see [pd distributions]

ELSE/gendyn~.body: [gendyn~] implements "Dynamic Stochastic Synthesis" based on Xenakis' 'GenDyn' algorithm. The waveform has a given number of points (linearly interpolated by default). The period of each point and its value changes according to a random walk (a.k.a. Brownian motion) with given distributions.

title: get~
ELSE/get~.description: get channels from a multichannel connection

messages.description: multichannel signal to get stuff from
one or more channels that you got

arguments.description: one or more channels to get

ELSE/get~.body: [get~] gets one or more channels from a multichannel connection. The channels are indexed from 1 (0 or less gives "none", the max channel number value is clipped to the input's channel size). You can give it lists to build or remap the signal arbitrarily like you'd do wtih dollar signs in a message.

ELSE/glide2.body: [glide2] generates a glide/portamento from its float input changes. The glide time in ms.

title: glide~
ELSE/glide~.body: [glide~] generates a glide/portamento from its signal input changes. The glide time in ms.

title: gmean
ELSE/gmean.body: [gmean] creates a list of geometric means. It takes a start point, an end point and the number of steps from start to end.

ELSE/grain.synth~.body: [grain.synth~] is a waveform based granular synthesizer that generates clouds of pitched grains.

ELSE/graph~.body: [graph~] is a very simple but convenient abstraction for visualizing audio signals.

title: gray~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/gray~.body: [gray~] generates noise based on "gray code" or reflected binary code (RBC), which results from flipping random bits (sot is is based on a pseudo random number generator algorithm.). "Gray Code" is named after Frank Gray, the owner of the patent of gray codes. This type of noise has a high RMS level relative to its peak to peak level. The spectrum is emphasized towards lower frequencies.

title: group

messages.description: calculate or output the last calculated value
when there's no remainder (group is empty)

ELSE/group.body: [group] groups messages according to a group size. When the input is smaller than the group size, it needs to reach the group size before it's sent out. If the input list is bigger than the group size, the remainder gets grouped in further lists.

title: hann~
ELSE/hann~.body: [hann~] is an abstraction that applies a Hann window into a signal input. the window size is the same as the block size. This is meant for FFT analysis.

title: henon~

messages.description: 2 floats set y[n-1] and y[n-2], respectively

ELSE/henon~.body:
[henon~] is a chaotic generator using the difference equation;
y[n] = 1 - a * y[n-1]^2 + b * y[n-2]

title: hex2dec

messages.description: hexadecimal value(s) to convert (outputs the result)
convert and output, or output the last converted value(s)
hexadecimal value to convert when receiving a bang

ELSE/hex2dec.body: [hex2dec] converts hexadecimal values to decimal ones. It takes symbols whose format allows lower and upper case letters and can also include "0x" or "0X" prefixes (necessary in the case of lists and arguments to avoid confusion with floats, which are ignored). List and symbol selectors aren't necessary.

title: highpass~

ELSE/hip.bw~.body: [hip.bw~] is a highpass butterworth filter abstraction based on [biquads~]. You can specify the cuttoff frequency and the order of the filter (from 2nd order to 100th order).

note on pitch to record into histogram

default: don't

export histogram table as a list
import histogram data as a list
non-0 embeds histogram data with the patch

ELSE/histogram.body: [histogram] records into a table how many times it received a positive integer number (treated as indexes, floats are truncated). A list input is treated as MIDI note messages and only note ons are recorded.

title: hot

messages.description: any messages go to the corresponding outlet

methods.description: set any input message without output

ELSE/hot.body: [hot] outputs messages when any inlet gets a message, making them all hot. Output is from right to left.

title: hz2rad
ELSE/hz2rad.body:
[hz2rad] converts a frequency in Hz to "Radians per Sample" - which depends on the patch's sample rate (sr). The conversion formula is;
rad = (hz * 2*pi / sr).

ELSE/impseq~.body: When receiving a signal trigger (0 to non-0 transition) or a bang, [impseq~] sends an impulse from a given sequence.

title: impulse
ELSE/impulse.body: This is much like the [impulse~] oscillator but operates in a control rate (with a resolution of 1 ms) and sends bangs. The difference between this and objects like [tempo] or [metro] is that a speed change takes effect right away.

imp2~

ELSE/impulse2~.body: A variant of [impulse~], [impulse2~] (or [imp2~] for short) is a two-sided impulse oscillator that accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for pulse width, phase sync and phase modulation.

imp~

ELSE/initmess.body: The [initmess] object sends messages when loading the patch. As an object, it can also deal with "$0" and arguments and expand them.

elements to insert int a message

ELSE/insert.body: [insert] inserts a message into a list according to an index. An index 0 will prepend the message. The object doesn't output a list selector, use [list] afterwards if you want it.

title: interpolate
ELSE/interpolate.body: [interpolate] allows you to use a GUI like a slider (or a MIDI controller) to interpolate between values. Whenever you change the target, you can interpolate to it by moving the slider to the opposite end.

number of lowest C
default: 3 - that is "C3

sets to toggle mode
default: non toggle

list MIDI Notes to be off with velocity of 0

sets toggle mode on <1> of off <0>

ELSE/keyboard.body: [keyboard] is a GUI that receives MIDI notes and also generates them from mouse clicking. Right click it for properties!

title: keycode

messages.description: 1 when key is pressed, 0 when released

ELSE/keycode.body: [keycode] outputs key codes from your keyboard based on their physical location, so it is layout independent (but please don't change layout after loading Pd and the object, this is problematic specially on Windows.. The right outlet outputs the code and the left output the key status (1 for pressed / 0 for released). The codes correspond to USB HID usage tables from <https://github.com/depp/keycode>.

title: keymap

messages.description: non-0 turns it on, 0 turns it off

arguments.description: number of octaves up or down
non zero sets to toggle mode

ELSE/keymap.body:
[keymap] maps your computer keyboard to MIDI pitches and generates note
on/off messages when keys get pressed and released. It works for any
keyboard layout. It does not work when in edit mode and autorepeated
keys get filtered.

title: keypress

messages.description: from learned key depending on the mode

methods.description: send stored input on right outlet

ELSE/keypress.body: [keypress] uses key presses of a single key to send a bang or a toggle. You can set the key symbol as an argument or ask the object to "learn" a pressed key (useful for kys like "shift" and stuff), which is then saved as an argument in the owning patch. Note that autorepeated keys are filtered! Key presses in edit mode are also filtered!

title: lace

title: lace~
ELSE/lace~.description: interleave two or more multichannel signals
ELSE/lace~.body: [lace~] interleaves two or more multichannel signals.

title: lag2~
ELSE/lag2~.body: [lag2~] is like [lag~] but has a different time for ramp up and ramp down. The lag time in ms is how long it takes for the signal to converge to within 0.01% of the target value. Note that the rising ramp is different than the descending ramp, unlike [glide2~].

title: lag~
ELSE/lag~.body: [lag~] is a one pole filter that creates an exponential glide/portamento for its signal input changes. The lag time in ms is how long it takes for the signal to converge to within 0.01% of the target value. Note that the rising ramp is different than the descending ramp, unlike [glide~]. the same kind of filter is used in other objects from the ELSE library (decay~/asr~/adsr~).

messages.description: stores value and outputs last value

methods.description: resets object and sets last value, no float sets to none

title: lastvalue~

messages.description: signal to detect the last value

ELSE/lastvalue~.body: [lastvalue~] reports the last value when the signal changes

title: latoocarfian~

messages.description: 2 floats set x[n-1] and y[n-1], respectively

title: lcm

messages.description: sets a list of numbers and output value
lcm of two or more floats

ELSE/lcm.body: [lcm] returns the least common multiple of two or more numbers. Floats are truncated to integers. Negative values are accepted, "0" is treated as "1". It has two inlets for setting two floats, but a list input to the left sets 2 or more floats to get their lcm.

title: level~
ELSE/level~.description: mono or multichannel dB level adjustment

messages.description: incoming signal(s) whose gain will be adjusted

ELSE/level~.body: [level~] is a convenient abstraction for adjusting a signal's gain in dB. At "12 o'clock" we have 'Unity Gain", which is 0 dB. Moving clockwise we linearly up to 12dB, whereas counterclockwise moves nonlinearly to -100, and reaching '-100' effectively means -inf dB (total silence). It has support for multichannel signals.

title: lfnoise

messages.description: frequency in Hz up to 100 (negative values accepted)
reset to a new random value
low frequency noise output in the range from 0 - 127

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/lfnoise.body: [lfnoise] is a control rate Low Frequency Noise that outputs random values. It doesn't need the DSP on to function. Give it a seed value if you want a reproducible output.

title: lfnoise~

messages.description: impulse forces a new random value
impulse forces a new random value

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/lfnoise~.body: [lfnoise~] is a low frequency (band limited) noise with or without interpolation. It generates random values (between -1 and 1) at a rate in Hz (negative frequencies accepted). It also has a 'sync' inlet that forces the object to generate a new random value when receiving an impulse. Use the seed message if you want a reproducible output.

title: lfo

messages.description: frequency in Hz up to 100 (negative values accepted)
sets waveform (sine, tri, saw, vsaw, and square)
refresh rate in ms (minimum is 5 ms)
LFO's waveform in the range from 0 - 127

flags.name: -square <float>
flags.description: sets waveform to square and width (from 0 to 1)

flags.name: -vsaw <float>
flags.description: sets waveform to variable sawtooth and width (from -1 to 1)

ELSE/lfo.body: [lfo] is a control rate LFO with some common waveforms. It doesn't need the DSP on to function,

title: limit

messages.description: any message input message to be speed limited
the last input in the time interval after last output

ELSE/limit.body: [limit] only allows messages through if a given time has elapsed since the previous input/output. Otherwise, it waits until that time passes and then sends the last received message since the previous output (ignoring the others).

title: lin2db
ELSE/lin2db.body: [lin2db] is a simple abstraction that converts amplitude values from linear to a deciBel Full Scale (dBFS). Conversion expression: dBFS = log10(amp) * 20, see the implementation inside the abstraction.

title: lin2db~
ELSE/lin2db~.body: [lin2db~] is a simple abstraction that converts amplitude values from linear to a deciBel Full Scale (dBFS). Conversion expression: dBFS = log10(amp) * 20, see the implementation inside the abstraction.

title: lincong~
ELSE/lincong~.body:
[lincong~] is a chaotic generator using the difference equation;
y[n] = (a * y[n-1] + c) % m - the output signal is scaled to a range of -1 to 1 automatically.

title: list.inc

messages.description: set start value and generate list
number of elements in the list

arguments.description: number of elements in the list

methods.description: nonzero sets to 'ratio' mode for given step

ELSE/list.inc.body: Use [list.inc] to generate a list by giving it a start value, a step value and number of elements. In 'ratio' mode the step is considered a ratio step that it multiplies to, otherwise it's just a regular linear addition.

title: list.seq

methods.description: go to an element index (indexed from 1)

ELSE/list.seq.body: Use [list.seq] to sequence through a list, looping through it.

title: loadbanger
lb
ELSE/loadbanger.description: bang messages at loading or when activated

messages.description: bang messages at loading or when activated

ELSE/loadbanger.body: [loadbanger] (or [lb]) sends "bangs" (from right to left) when loading the patch and also sends bangs when receiving any message or clicked on. The number of outputs is defined by the argument (1 by default). [lb] can also be useful for dynamic patching (for creating inlets/outlets) in init mode.

title: logistic~
ELSE/logistic~.body: [logistic~] is a chaotic generator based on the difference equation: y[n] = (3 + p) * y[n-1] * (1- y[n-1]).

title: loop

a float sets number of iterations, a list sets start/end and step (optionally)
sets the increment value of the counter
continues the loop after being paused

ELSE/loop.body: [loop] is a loop counter, but it can also loop bangs like [until] (using the -b flag). A float sets number of count iteration upwards. A list with two elements sets a start and end value (and allows counting upwards or downwards). A counter step value can be set with a third optional element in a list.

title: lop.bw~

arguments.description: order of the filter from 2 to 100

ELSE/lop.bw~.body: [lop.bw~] is a lowpass butterworth filter abstraction based on [biquads~]. You can specify the cutoff frequency and the order of the filter (from 2nd order to 100th order).

title: lorenz~

messages.description: 3 floats set x[n-1], y[n-1] and z[n-1] respectively

methods.description: clears values of z, y and z

ELSE/lorenz~.body: The [lorenz~] is a strange attractor discovered by Edward N. Lorenz while studying mathematical models of the atmosphere.

title: lowpass~

flags.name: -bw
flags.description: sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

methods.description: clears filter's memory if you blow it up
sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

ELSE/lowpass~.body: [lowpass~] is a 2nd order lowpass resonant filter.
