title: mag

messages.description: converts the last received coordinates pair
real part from the Cartesian coordinates
imaginary part from the Cartesian coordinates

arguments.description: power" sets to power magnitude spectrum

ELSE/mag.body: [mag] gets the spectrum magnitudes (amplitudes) from Cartesian coordinates (real / imaginary). This is much like the amplitude output of [car2pol], but you can also get the power magnitudes instead with the 1st argument.

title: mag~

messages.description: real part from the Cartesian coordinates
imaginary part from the Cartesian coordinates

arguments.description: power" sets to power magnitude spectrum

ELSE/mag~.body:
[mag~] gets the spectrum magnitudes (amplitudes) from Cartesian coordinates (real / imaginary). This is much like the amplitude output of [car2pol~], but you can also get the power magnitudes instead with the 1st argument.
This object is useful for spectral processing that doesn't require the phase values.

title: makenote2

messages.description: with at least pitch, velocity and duration in beats

ELSE/makenote2.body: Similarly to [makenote], [makenote2] takes a list with pitch, velocity and duration. Extra information is possible in this list, like a float for MIDI channel or whatever, but a list with at least 3 elements is necessary. The duration is in beats and the BPM is set via the argument or right inlet. The first item on the list (pitch) can be a symbol (like 'C4'). If you want the duration to specify a value in ms, use '60.000' as the bpm value!

title: markov

messages.description: get value from Markov chain and goes to the next

methods.description: create Markov chain from memory and go to start
go to the start of the chain
go to a random position in the chain
a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/markov.body: [markov] creates Markov chains of any order out of progressions of floats, symbols or lists (which can be used to create polyphonic chains). You can change the order and recreate the chain. You can keep feeding information after the chain was created and recreate with the new information. You can also clear the memory to restart from scratch. State saving is possible with the 2nd argument, where the chain is saved with the patch.

title: match~

messages.description: one or more floats reset argument values
impulse when signal matches an argument
impulse when signal doesn't match arguments

ELSE/match~.body:
[match~] is quite similar to pd vanilla's [select] object, but is signal based and sends impulses instead of bangs.
When a signal value corresponds to one of the arguments, an impulse is sent to its corresponding outlet. Otherwise, the rightmost outlet sends an impulse.

title: maxpeak~

messages.description: maximum peak value so far in dB

methods.description: non zero sets to linear output

ELSE/maxpeak~.body: [maxpeak~] returns the maximum peak amplitude so far in dB. A bang in the right inlet resets the maximum value. The right outlet sends bangs when the input is clipped (exceeds 0 dB).

title: median

messages.description: the float list to calculate the median on
the median of the input list

ELSE/median.body: The [median] objects returns the median of a number list. Non float elements are ignored.

title: median~

messages.description: the signal to perform the median on
number of samples to perform the median
the median of the input signal

arguments.description: number of samples to perform the median

ELSE/median~.body: The [median~] objects returns the median of a number of samples (minimum number of samples is 1 and maximum is the block size).

title: merge

messages.description: message composed of the merged messages

ELSE/merge.body: [merge] takes any type of messages in any inlet and combines them into a message.

title: merge~
ELSE/merge~.body: [merge~] merges different signals of different channel lenghts. The number of inlets is set via the argument.

title: message
ELSE/message.body: The [message] object stores and outputs any kind of message in Pd. As an object, $1/$2 loads arguments when used in an abstraction, so the syntax is not the same as a message box. Nonetheless, it deals with comma and semicolons in the same way as regular message boxes do. See [pd syntax] for more details.

title: messbox

messages.description: sets messages with "$1" "$2", etc and outputs them

ELSE/messbox.body: The [messbox] object is an object you can type in messages while in run mode. It's like atom boxes but does all that a message box does. Any message type is possible. It deals with dollar signs, comma and semicolons in the same way as regular message boxes. See [pd syntax] for more details.

title: meter
ELSE/meter.body: [meter] is a CPU meter based on the "Load Meter" patch found in Pd's Media menu.

title: meter2~

messages.description: left incoming signal channels to be vu-metered
right incoming signal channels to be vu-metered
left incoming signals are passed through
right incoming signals are passed through
vu values (RMS/peak amplitude in dBFS) of inputs

ELSE/meter2~.body: [meter2~] is a convenient stereo VU-meter abstraction based on [vu~] and vanilla's [vu] GUI. See also [meter~], [meter4~] and [meter8~].

title: meter4~

messages.description: incoming signal channels to be vu-metered
vu values (RMS/peak amplitude in dBFS) of inputs

ELSE/meter4~.body: [meter4~] is a convenient quadraphonic VU-meter abstraction based on [vu~] and vanilla's [vu] GUI - see also: [meter~], [meter2~] and [meter8~].

title: meter8~

messages.description: incoming signal channels to be vu-metered
vu values (RMS/peak amplitude in dBFS) of inputs

ELSE/meter8~.body: [meter8~] is a convenient octaphonic VU-meter abstraction based on [vu~] and vanilla's [vu] GUI - see also: [meter~], [meter2~] and [meter4~].

title: meter~

messages.description: incoming mono signal to be vu-metered

ELSE/meter~.body: [meter~] is a convenient mono VU-meter abstraction based on [vu~] and vanilla's [vu] GUI object. See also [meter2~], [meter4~] and [meter8~].

title: metronome

messages.description: actual beat length, actual tempo and bar duration in ms

methods.description: set time signature symbol and group value

ELSE/metronome.body: [metronome] understands complex time signatures and outputs timeline data (bar, sub-bar, beat and sub-beat count) and phase output. It also syncs to [clock] objects.

title: metronome~
ELSE/metronome~.body: [metronome~] works in conjunction with [metronome] and outputs metronome clicks.

title: midi.clock

messages.description: sets BPM and also sets to internal clock
time interval in BPM from external source

arguments.description: sets BPM and also sets to internal clock

ELSE/midi.clock.body:
[midi.clock] is a MIDI clock abstraction. It works with an internal or external clock (from an external MIDI device). It sends a bang at each MIDI clock and also sends a time interval (in BPM) from external clock sources.
By default, [clock] is set to an external input, which comes from the MIDI device specified in Pd's MIDI configuration. But if you give it a float argument, it sets to internal clock at the BPM specified by the float.

title: midi.in
ELSE/midi.in.body: [midi.in] sends "cooked" MIDI data instead of "raw" data like [midiin] with MIDI data type symbol, values and channel (but a note output is sent as a simple numeric list). It can take a raw MIDI input via its inlet but it also listens to your connected MIDI device.

title: midi

messages.description: raw MIDI data stream from a MIDI file
sent at the end of a sequence

arguments.description: a MIDI or text file to load

flags.name: -loop
flags.description:
sets the loop mode
default: no loop

methods.description: sets to synced mode and expects clock ticks (bangs)
stops recording/playing and goes back to start
sets a reading speed in % of original
outputs the MIDI data stream at once

ELSE/midi.body: [midi] plays/records raw MIDI streams and can save/read MIDI files or import/export to txt files.

title: midi.learn

methods.description: print send stored input on right outlet

ELSE/midi.learn.body:
[midi.learn] is an abstraction based on [savestate] that learns and saves any MIDI input data. Activate it via bang or click and send it MIDI data so it learns where it comes from. The information gets stored in the owning patch.
The Learned control/program change are followed by numbers that specify 'control/program number' and 'channel' or just a 'channel number' for note and bend messages (touch and polytouch are not supported).

title: midi.out

flags.name: -font <symbol>
flags.description: default 'dejavu sans mono' or 'menlo' for mac

ELSE/midi.out.body: [midi.in] sends MIDI data from "cooked" data, instead of "raw" data like [midiout]. It takes data type symbol, values and channel (but a note output is received as a simple numeric list). It outputs raw MIDI data but it also sends out to the connected MIDI device.

title: midi2freq
ELSE/midi2freq.body: [midi2freq] converts MIDI to frequency like [mtof], but it also converts lists and allows you so set a different frequency reference value for A4 so you can explore different tunings. Note that 'MIDI cents' are possible, where 69.5 is 50 cents (or a quarter tone) higher than 69 (hence 1 cent = 0.01).

title: midi2note
ELSE/midi2note.description: Convert MIDI pitch to note name
ELSE/midi2note.body: [midi2note] converts a MIDI pitch value to note names (such as Eb3). The names end with an octave number, in a way that middle C (MIDI pitch = 60) represents C4 represents. Float inputs are rounded to integers.

title: mix2~
ELSE/mix2~.body: [mix2~] is a convenient 2 channel mixer abstraction with gain and panning.

title: mix4~
ELSE/mix4~.body: [mix4~] is a convenient 4 channel mixer abstraction with gain and panning.

title: mono

methods.description: non-0 - legato mode, zero - restores default
sends a note off for the hanging note and clears memory

ELSE/mono.body: [mono] takes note messages and handles them to emulate monophonic synth behavior. Its internal memory can remember up to the last 10 input voices *and pitches need to be >= 0).

title: mono.rev~
ELSE/mono.rev~.body: [mono.rev~] is a reverb abstraction with mono input but stereo output. It's based on a feedback delay network like vanilla's [rev3~] object, but it offers a "room size" parameter whose typical value is around 0.5, values closer to 0 and 1 may create unusual results. Changing size values may generates unusual artifacts (you can clear the delay buffers when you do it to prevent it, but you may get clicks).

title: mono~

arguments.description: non zero sets to legato mode

methods.description: non zero sets to legato mode, zero restores default
sends a note off for the hanging note and clears memory

ELSE/mono~.body: [mono~] emulates monophonic synth behaviour and is much like [mono], but can also glide the pitch output with a portamento setting.

title: morph

messages.description: list with one or more elements to morph to

ELSE/morph.body: [morph] is an abstraction based on vanilla's [line], but besides floats, it also works for lists - so you can transition (or morph) between them. It takes a time to reach the target and a grain time just like [line].

title: morph~
ELSE/morph~.body: [morph~] allows you to make a spectral crossfade between the amplitudes and phases of two inputs. You can then do cross synthesis and spectral morphing.

title: mouse
ELSE/mouse.body: [mouse] grabs mouse interaction (click and coordinates). Click and see. This works for any click Pd receives in any window, not only this patch's.

title: mov.avg

messages.description: sets new number of values and clears

ELSE/mov.avg.body: [mov.avg] gives you a running/moving average over the last 'n' values.

title: mov.avg~

messages.description: number of last samples to apply the average to
the moving average over the last 'n' samples

methods.description: sets new maximum size and clears filter's memory

ELSE/mov.avg~.body: [mov.avg~] gives you a signal running/moving average over the last 'n' given samples. This is also a type of lowpass filter.

title: mov.rms~

messages.description: number of last samples to apply the average to
the RMS over the time window

arguments.description: sets number of samples in the window

flags.name: -lin
flags.description:
sets output to linear
default: dBFS

methods.description: sets new maximum size and clears buffer's memory
non zero sets to linear output

ELSE/mov.rms~.body: [mov.rms~] gives you a running/moving RMS (Root Mean Square) over a time window of samples. At each sample, the RMS of the last 'n' sample is given.

title: mpe.in

messages.description: set port number to listen to (0 means all ports)

voice number, type and data
end:

flags.name: -ext
flags.description:
only listen to external input source
default: log

methods.description: only listen to external input source if nonzero

ELSE/mpe.in.body: [mpe.in] sends "cooked" MPE data from external "raw" MIDI data or an internally connected device. It outputs most common MIDI messages prepended with a voice number indexed from 0 (which depends on MIDI channel in this case).

title: ms2samps

messages.description: converted value in number of samples

ELSE/ms2samps.body:
[ms2samps] is a simple abstraction that converts time values from ms to number of samples.
Conversion expression: samps = ms * samplerate/1000.

title: ms2samps~

messages.description: converted value in number of samples

ELSE/ms2samps~.body:
[ms2samps] is a simple abstraction that converts time values from ms to number of samples. 
Conversion expression: samps = ms * samplerate/1000.

title: mtx.ctl

messages.description: sets and outputs column, row and status

methods.description: sets column, row and status (no output)
non-0 save internal contents with the object

ELSE/mtx.ctl.body: [mtx.ctl] provides a user matrix interface control. The cells in the grid can be either on or off. The output is a list with 3 floats that specify: column, row and status.

title: mtx.mc~

messages.description: 3 floats to set: channel input, output and gain

methods.description: prints state off all cells in pd window

ELSE/mtx.mc~.body: [mtx.mc~] routes a channel signal from a multichannel input to one or more channels of a multichannel output.

title: mtx~
ELSE/mtx~.body: [mtx~] routes signals from any inlets to one or more outlets. If more than one inlet connects to an outlet, the output is the sum of the inlets' signals. Use [mtx.ctl] to control it.

title: multi.vsl

messages.description: outputs all slider's values (depends on mode)
sets and dumps sliders values from the first (0)
slider number / slider value in the default mode or all values as a list in the "list mode
list of values when receiving export message

flags.name: -n <float>
flags.description:
sets number of sliders size
default: 8

flags.name: -jump <float>
flags.description:
non-0 sets jump on click mode
default: 0

flags.name: -savestate <float>
flags.description:
non-0 will save object state
default: 0

flags.name: -bgcolor <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets background color in RGB
default: 255 255 255

flags.name: -fgcolor <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets foreground color in RGB
default: 220 220 220

flags.name: -linecolor <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets line color in RGB
default: 0 0 0

flags.name: -mode <float>
flags.description:
non zro sets to 'list mode'
default: 0

methods.description: outputs values sequentially as slider number / value
sets values from the index defined in the first float
gets sliders values from the first float
non-0 sets jump on click mode
sets number of sliders and values (and dumps them)
outputs sliders values as a list

ELSE/multi.vsl.body: [multi.vsl] is a multi slider GUI abstraction.

title: nchs~
ELSE/nchs~.description: get number of channels from multi-channel connections

messages.description: signal to query number of channels

ELSE/nchs~.body: [nchs~] gets the number of channels from a multi-channel connection, as provided by [snake~ in], [clone] or other objects. The object outputs values whenever the DSP chain gets updated and you can also query for the number of channels with a bang message. It also works in subpatches or abstractions because [inlet~] is also multi-channel aware.

title: nmess

messages.description: resets and sets 'n' number of messages
output a message if the gate is opened
output a message if the gate is closed

ELSE/nmess.body: [nmess] allows 'n' number of messages to be gated through the left outlet (it goes to the right outlet otherwise). A bang in the right inlet resets and reopens the gate.

title: noisegate~

flags.name: -size <float>
flags.description:
sets analysis window size in samples
default: 512

ELSE/noisegate~.body: [noisegate~] is a noise gate abstraction. It takes a threshold in dBFS in which it only audio through that has a RMS value over that threshold.

title: nop~
ELSE/nop~.body: [nop~] does no operation, that is, it does nothing. It's mostly useful for managing singal cords in your patch.

title: norm~

flags.name: -size <float>
flags.description:
sets analysis window size in samples
default: 1024

ELSE/norm~.body: [norm~] is a normalizer abstraction based on [mov.rms~]. It takes a normalization value in dBFS and a window analysis size.

title: note.in

flags.name: -rel
flags.description: sets the object to output release velocity and note on/off flag

flags.name: -ext
flags.description: only listen to external input source

flags.name: -both
flags.description: sets the object to output both note on and off velocity

methods.description: nonzero sets to listen only to external input source

ELSE/note.in.body: [note.in] extracts MIDI Pitch information from external "raw" MIDI data or an internally connected device. It deals with both NoteOn and NoteOff messages (with release velocity).

title: note

flags.name: -font <symbol>
flags.description: default 'dejavu sans mono' or 'menlo' for mac

methods.description: appends a message to the note
prepends a message to the note
sets RGB text color (values from 0 to 255)
sets RGB background color (values from 0 to 255)

ELSE/note.body: [note] is a GUI meant only to display text notes. This is basically the same of Pd's comment but with the advantage of being able to set the font, size, color, background color, bold, italic, underline and justification.

title: note.out

flags.name: -rel
flags.description: sets the object to output release velocity and note on/off flag

flags.name: -both
flags.description: sets the object to output both note on and off velocity

ELSE/note.out.body: [note.out] formats and sends raw MIDI pitch messages to Pd's MIDI output and its outlet. An argument sets channel number (the default is 1).

title: note2midi
ELSE/note2midi.description: convert note name to MIDI pitch

messages.description: note name to convert to MIDI pitch

ELSE/note2midi.body: [note2midi] converts note names (such as 'C4') to MIDI pitch values. All enharmonic names are included (even unusual ones like Cb and double sharps/flats). It takes symbols, lists or anythings that end with an octave number, in a way that C4 represents middle C (MIDI pitch = 60). Range is from C0 to B8.

title: notedur2ratio
ELSE/notedur2ratio.description: convert note duration to fraction or float

messages.description: in note duration syntax to convert
converted duration to fraction or float

flags.name: -f
flags.description:
sets output to float
default: fraction

ELSE/notedur2ratio.body: [notedur2ratio] converts from a note duration syntax to a fractional notation or a float. The syntax is: '1n' for whole note, '2n' for half note, '4n' for quarter note, '8n' for eighth note (and so on down to 128n). Dotted notes are possible and you can add tuplets to the symbol in the [x:y] format or you can add the following letters to specify most common tuplets: 'd' for duplet, 't' for triplet, 'q' for quintuplet, 's' for septuplet and 'n' for nonuplet. Tied notes are also possible with "_". Details in the examples below.

title: noteinfo

messages.description: resets by sending note-offs and erasing events' memory
voice number (from 0), event number, pitch, velocity, duration (delta time for note on and duration for note off)

ELSE/noteinfo.body: [noteinfo] sends number of active voices in the right outlet and a list of MIDI note information to the left.

title: numbox~

messages.description: input when monitoring or generated values instead

flags.name: -load <float>
flags.description: sets initialization and generating value with given float or sets current value if no float is given.

flags.name: -bgcolor <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets background color in RGB
default: 255 255 255

flags.name: -fgcolor <f, f, f>
flags.description:
sets frontground color in RGB
default: 0 0 0

flags.name: -ramp <float>
flags.description:
sets ramp time in ms (minimum 0)
default: 10

flags.name: -rate <float>
flags.description:
sets refresh rate in ms (minimum 15)
default: 100

methods.description: sets initialization and generating value with given float or sets current value if no float is given
sets minimum and maximum dragging values

ELSE/numbox~.body: [numbox~] is a signal GUI number box. It can be used to display/monitor signal values at a fixed rate or generate them with a ramp time.

title: nyquist~

methods.description: set and get the Nyquist frequency in Hz
set and get the Nyquist frequency in kHz
set and get the Nyquist period in ms
set and get the Nyquist period in seconds

ELSE/nyquist~.body: [nyquist~] reports the Nyquist (which is half the sample rate) as a frequency or period. It sends it when loading the patch, when receiving a bang or when the sample rate changes. It reports it either in Hz or kHz and the period either in seconds or milliseconds. like [sr~], it doesn't report up/down sampling rates.

title: op

arguments.description: operator: see [pd set] for all options

methods.description: operator see [pd set] for all options

ELSE/op.body: [op] provides many operators and is most useful for lists, as there are objects in Pd Vanilla for all these operations.

title: openfile

flags.name: -h <anything>
flags.description: sets to hyperlink (GUI) mode, 1st element is file to open and name to display, further arguments overwrite display.

methods.description: opens folder/file/weblink specified by the symbol, if no symbol is given, the current folder is opened

ELSE/openfile.body: [openfile] can be used to open folders and files in your computer and also weblinks.

title: op~
ELSE/op~.body: [op~] is a signal operator (either comparison or bitwise), which is defined by the first argument. This is more efficient than using [expr~] (which also contains all these operators).

title: order

messages.description: any number of elements to be split and ordered
ordered lists, with the split elements preceded by index

arguments.description: number of elements to be ordered

methods.description: number of elements in each ordered list

ELSE/order.body: [order] splits a list into successive lists of a given size (default 1 element) and tags each of them with ordered indexes. This is useful for sending values to all instances of an object loaded in [clone]!

title: osc.format
ELSE/osc.format.body: [osc.format] is similar to Vanilla's [oscformat]. This is still way too simple and experimental. It is used in the [osc.send] abstraction and can be used for edge and lower level cases. It takes anything messages or lists whose first element is an address that start with "/". The type of data for each element in the message is either a float or a string only depending on the atom type, so there are only these options (so far). You can't send bundles either. Use the original Vanilla [oscformat] or mrpeach's packOSC object for more complete objects.

title: osc.parse
ELSE/osc.parse.body: [osc.parse] is similar to Vanilla's [oscparse] but the output is not a list and more closely related on how OSC messages are generally dealt with. It is still in the [osc.receive] abstraction and you can use the object for more edge and lower level cases.

title: osc.receive

arguments.description: port to connect to at load time

ELSE/osc.receive.body: [osc.receive] receives OSC messages from network connections and is an abstraction based on [osc.parse] and [netreceive].

title: osc.route

messages.description: routed messages that correspond to an argument

arguments.description: list of OSC addresses to route to

ELSE/osc.route.body: [osc.route] routes OSC messages received from [osc.receive]. It follows the same logic as Vanilla's [route] where arguments set the number of outlets and an extra is created for no matches. The difference is that it manages OSC addresses instead, specified with the "/" separator. Note how the '/hz' address matches input messages that start with '/hz' even if it has further sub addresses.

title: osc.send

arguments.description: address/port to connect to at load time

ELSE/osc.send.body: [osc.send] sends OSC messages over the network and is an abstraction based on [osc.format] and [netsend].

title: oscbank~

flags.name: -partial <list>
flags.description:
sets list of partials for all oscillators
default: all 1

flags.name: -amp <list>
flags.description:
sets list of amplitudes for all oscillators
default: all 1

flags.name: -phase <list>
flags.description:
sets list of phases for all oscillators
default: all 0

flags.name: -ramp <list>
flags.description: sets list of ramp times for all oscillators

flags.name: -freq <float>
flags.description:
sets fundamental frequency in Hz
default: 0

flags.name: -rampall <float>
flags.description:
sets a ramp time for all oscillators
default: all 10

methods.description: list of partials for all oscillators
list of amplitudes for all oscillators
list of phases (0-1) for all oscillators
list of ramp time for all oscillators
sets ramp time for all oscillators

ELSE/oscbank~.body: [oscbank~] is a bank made of [sine~] objects. You can set any number of oscillators and control their parameters. Unlike [oscbank2~], you have a fundamental frequency input and the frequency of each oscillator is specified as a ratio of that frequency. If you use flags, the number of elements in the list (such as amplitude list) sets the number of oscillators in the bank, and you must not use regular arguments in this case.

title: oscnoise~

arguments.description: frequency in midi pitch or hertz

flags.name: -midi <float>
flags.description: sets frequency input in MIDI pitch (default hertz)

methods.description: non zero sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch

ELSE/oscnoise~.body: [oscnoise~] is an oscillator that accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for phase sync and phase modulation. The waveform is a table filled with white noise. A bang updates the noise table.

title: out.mc~

messages.description: incoming signal routed to mono output
incoming signal routed to right channel output: [dac~ 2]

methods.description: turns DSP Engine (Compute Audio) On or Off
sets ramp time in ms for slider values (default 20)

ELSE/out.mc~.body: [out.mc~] takes multichannel signals and outputs them from given [dac~] channel output (default 1). It has controls for mute, DSP on/off, ramp time, maximum gain and scaling mode.

title: out4~

methods.description: turns DSP Engine (Compute Audio) On or Off
sets ramp time in ms for slider values (default 20)

ELSE/out4~.body: [out4~] is a convenient quadraphonic input/output abstraction. It has controls for mute, DSP on/off, ramp time, maximum gain and scaling mode. Note that you need to specify at least 4 output channels in preferences => audio.

title: out8~

methods.description: turns DSP Engine (Compute Audio) On or Off
sets ramp time in ms for slider values (default 20)

ELSE/out8~.body: [out8~] is a convenient octaphonic input/output abstraction. It has controls for mute, DSP on/off, ramp time, maximum gain and scaling mode. Note that you need to specify at least 8 output channels in preferences => audio.

title: out~

messages.description: incoming signal routed to left channel output: [dac~ 1]
incoming signal routed to right channel output: [dac~ 2]

methods.description: turns DSP Engine (Compute Audio) On or Off
sets ramp time in ms for slider values (default 20)

ELSE/out~.body: [out~] is a convenient mono/stereo input and stereo output abstraction. It has controls for mute, DSP on/off, ramp time, maximum gain and scaling mode.

title: pack2

messages.description: outputs the stored list of elements
a float or symbol to set to slot 0 and output
a float or symbol to set to slot 0
a float or symbol to set to slot $nth and output
a float or symbol to set to slot $nth
the message composed of the given elements

arguments.description: the number of elements = the number of inlets. <f> or <symbol> — initial value

ELSE/pack2.body: [pack2] is kinda similar to Pd Vanilla's [pack], but any inlet triggers the output (though a "set" message avoids the output). It can also initialize a symbol value and it doesn't create a list selector for you. Moreover, you can change many elements with a list message and an element that was initialized as a float can become a symbol and vice versa.

title: pad

messages.description: report mouse button press <1> and release <0>

flags.name: -dim <f, f>
flags.description:
set horizontal and vertical dimensions
default: 127 127

ELSE/pad.body: [pad] is a GUI object that reports mouse coordinates over its area and click status. If you click and drag, you can get values outside the GUI area.

title: pan.mc~
ELSE/pan.mc~.description: circular panning over given number of channels

flags.name: -radians
flags.description: change 3rd inlet input from degrees to radians

ELSE/pan.mc~.body: [pan.mc~] pans an input signal to 'n' specified channels in a multichannel output with equal power crossfading between adjacent channels according to a spread parameter. The speakers are supposedly disposed in a circular setting with equidistant angles. The output selection can then be considered an azimuth angle input whose range is normalized from 0 to 1, where 1 goes back to 0!

title: panic

messages.description: sends note off for hanging notes

methods.description: clears the hanging notes that [midiflush] keeps track of

ELSE/panic.body: Like a "panic button", [panic] keeps track of raw Note-on messages that weren't switched off and "flushes" them by sending corresponding note-offs when it receives a bang.

title: pan~
ELSE/pan~.description: circular panning over given number of channels

flags.name: -radians
flags.description: change 3rd inlet input from degrees to radians

ELSE/pan~.body: [pan~] pans an input signal to 'n' specified outlets with equal power crossfading between adjacent channels according to a spread parameter. The speakers are supposedly disposed in a circular setting with equidistant angles. The output selection can then be considered an azimuth angle input whose range is normalized from 0 to 1, where 1 goes back to 0!

title: parabolic~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description: sets frequency input in MIDI pitch

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: nonzero sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
<channel, freq> set a single frequency channel
nonzero sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/parabolic~.body: [parabolic~] is a parabolic oscillator that accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for phase sync and phase modulation. A parabolic waveform is similar to a sinusoid but it is not band limited (has aliasing).

title: pattern

messages.description: non-0 starts, zero stops the sequencer
bang or index float at every new note

flags.name: -i
flags.description:
sets to output index
default: bang

ELSE/pattern.body: [pattern] is a rhythmic pattern sequencer. The note durations are specified as floats or fractions (where '1' is a whole note), negative values are rests! A '|' separates into different bars. When you set a new pattern via the right inlet, it only starts when the previous one ends.

title: pdlink
ELSE/pdlink.description: send messages across network by name
ELSE/pdlink.body: [pd.link] allows you to communicate to/from different Pd instances over local network, as different versions and even forks of Pure Data (such as plugdata). It works like [send]/[receive] (or [else/sender]/[else/receiver]) as it just needs a symbol address. It works via network but it's simpler as it doesn't require network or OSC configuration! It also allows you to communicate to a [pd~] subprocess.

title: pdlink~
ELSE/pdlink~.description: send messages across network by name

flags.name: -compress
flags.description: apply Opus compression to audio stream

ELSE/pdlink~.body: [pd.link~] allows you to send/receive audio streans to/from different Pd instances, as different versions, platforms and even forks of Pure Data (such as plugdata). It works like [send~]/[receive~] as it just needs a symbol address. It works via network but it's simpler as it doesn't require network configuration! It also allows you to communicate to a [pd~] subprocess. You can make up to 16 sends to a single receive, but you can receive as many times as you like.

title: peak~

flags.name: -lin
flags.description:
sets the output to linear
default: dBFS

methods.description: sets window and hop size in samples
non zero sets to linear output

ELSE/peak~.body: [peak~] is similar to Pd Vanilla's [env~], but it reports the peak amplitude value in linear (default) or dBFS.

title: perlin~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/perlin~.body: [perlin~] is an abstraction that implements 1-dimensional Perlin Noise (a type of gradient noise developed by Ken Perlin). It uses [white~] as a noise source into a sample and hold function and generates smoothened functions according to a frequency value in Hz (values under 0 and above Nyquist are aliased).

title: pgm.in

flags.name: -ext
flags.description:
only listen to external input source
default: no

methods.description: non zero sets to listen only to external input source

ELSE/pgm.in.body: [pgm.in] extracts MIDI Program information from raw MIDI input (such as from [midiin]). Unlike vanilla's [pgmin] and [pgmout], the program change values are from 0 to 127!

title: pgm.out
ELSE/pgm.out.body: [pgm.out] formats and sends raw MIDI program messages. An argument sets channel number (the default is 1). Unlike vanilla's [pgmin] and [pgmout], the program change values are from 0 to 127!

title: phaser~
ELSE/phaser~.body: [phaser~] is a mono phaser effect abstraction. You can set the number of stages with the first argument, which sets the order of the internal allpass filter (needs to be a multiple of 2). It requires a signal LFO to control the frequency sweep.

title: phaseseq~
ELSE/phaseseq~.body: [phaseseq~] takes a list of thresholds and outputs impulses when reaching the threshold values.

title: phasor
ELSE/phasor.body: This is like the [phasor~] but operates in a control rate (with a maximum resolution of 1 ms).

title: pi

messages.description: calculate or output the last calculated value

ELSE/pi.body: [pi] calculates and outputs the value of pi. It receives a multiplier value via the argument or second inlet, which needs to be greater than 0 (otherwise it's considered as 1).

title: pic

messages.description: open an image file named by the symbol
a bang is output if you click on the image
pic width/height when in report size mode

flags.name: -open <symbol>
flags.description:
sets file name to open
default: empty

flags.name: -outline
flags.description:
sets to outline mode
default: no outline

flags.name: -size
flags.description:
sets to "report size" mode
default: no report

methods.description: open an image file named by the symbol
same as open, but pd doesn't ask to save changes
non-0 sets to report size mode

ELSE/pic.body: [pic] loads image pictures that you can interact with. It only works with file types: .gif, .ppm & .pgm. you click on the picture and it sends a bang (default) or 1 when clicking and 0 when releasing (when in latch mode).

title: pick

messages.description: the element from the input message

ELSE/pick.body: [pick] picks an element from an input message according to an element number. Negative values count from last to the first element. If you ask for an element number out of the range, nothing is output.

title: pick~
ELSE/pick~.description: pick a channel from a multichannel connection
ELSE/pick~.body: [pick~] picks a channel from a multichannel connection. The channel is specified via argument or float input and is indexed from 1 (0 gives "none"). Negative values count from last to the first element. If you ask for an element number out of the range, you also get "none".

title: pimp

messages.description: output a bang at period transitions

ELSE/pimp.body: This is like the [pimp~] but operates in a control rate (with a maximum resolution of 1 ms).

title: pimpmul~
ELSE/pimpmul~.body: [pimpmul~] is a "pimp~" multiplier. The [pimp~] object from ELSE is both a [phasor~] and an [imp~] oscillator. Use [pimpul~] to keep [phasor~] or [pimp~] objects in sync with different frequency ratios.

title: pimp~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description:
sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
default: Hz

flags.name: -soft
flags.description:
sets to soft sync mode
default: hard

methods.description: non-0 sets to frequency input in MIDI pitch
non-0 sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/pimp~.body: [pimp~] is a combination of [phasor~] and [imp~] (alias of [impulse~]). It's [imp~] with an extra phase output (left outlet). The impulse happens at every phase period start (in both negative or positive frequencies/directions). It also has inlets for phase sync and phase modulation like [imp~].

title: ping.pong~
ELSE/ping.pong~.body: [ping.pong~] is a ping pong delay object

title: pink~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/pink~.body: [pink~] is a pink noise generator, which sounds less hissy than white noise (but not as less as brown~). White noise has constant spectral power, but pink noise has constant power per octave and it decrease 3dB per octave. Like other noise objects, this is based on a pseudo random number generator algorithm.

title: pipe2
ELSE/pipe2.body: [pipe2] is similar to vanilla's pipe. It takes all kinds of messages and delays them.

title: pitch.shift~
ELSE/pitch.shift~.body: [pitch.shift~] is a pitch shifter abstraction based on granulation. You can set a transposition factor in cents and a grain size in ms.

title: midi2note
ELSE/pitch2note.description: Convert MIDI pitch to note name
ELSE/pitch2note.body: [midi2note] converts a MIDI pitch value to note names (such as Eb3). The names end with an octave number, in a way that middle C (MIDI pitch = 60) represents C4 represents. Float inputs are rounded to integers.

title: plaits~

messages.description: level for built-in VCA or excitation signal
output info on setting model or 'dump'

flags.name: -cv/-midi
flags.description:
set to pitch input in CV, MIDI
default: Hz

flags.name: -trigger
flags.description:
set to trigger mode
default: regular

ELSE/plaits~.body: [plaits~] is based on the "Plaits" module from Mutable Instruments.

title: plate.rev~
ELSE/plate.rev~.body: [plate.rev~] is a reverb abstraction based on a patch by Tom Erbe implementing the plate reverb model by Dattorro. It has a mono input and stereo output.

title: play.file~

messages.description: a bang when finishing playing the sample

arguments.default: 1 if no file is given, or sound file's if given

arguments.description: the name of the file to open

methods.description: opens a file with the symbol name (no symbol opens dialog box)
sets a file to open next time it starts playing

ELSE/play.file~.body: [play.file~] is a convenient abstraction based on [readsf~] to read files from your computer. Note that the file sample needs to be at the same sample rate as Pd's. The object can open files with absolute paths and is also able to search for files relative to the parent patch.

title: player~

messages.description: a bang when finishing playing the buffer

arguments.default: 1 if no file is given, or sound file's if given

arguments.description: the name of the file to open

methods.description: opens a file (no symbol opens dialog box)
sets a file to open (needs a reload message)
sets start and end point proportionally (from 0 to 1)
sets position (from 0 to 1) and starts playing it
if no float is given, plays whole file. 1st float sets start, 2nd sets end (in ms) and 3rd sets speed rate
stops playing and outputs 0 (cannot be resumed)
pauses at a particular point (can be resumed)

ELSE/player~.body: [player~] is a convenient abstraction based on [soundfiler] and [tabplayer~]. It's much more versatile than [play.file~]. For instance, you can play in different speeds and backwards.

title: pluck~

messages.description: set frequency and trigger value (normalized to MIDI range)

flags.name: -in
flags.description: creates an extra right inlet for noise input

methods.description: non zero sets to MIDI pitch input instead of hz.

ELSE/pluck~.body: [pluck~] is a Karplus-Strong algorithm with a 1st order lowpass filter in the feedback loop. It takes frequency in hertz or midi, a decay time in ms and a cutoff frequency in hz for the filter. It is triggered by signals at zero to non zero transitions or by lists at control rate.

title: pm2~

methods.description: set index ('1to2' sets op1 to op2 index, etc)
set op ratio ('ratio1' sets op1, etc)
set op detuning ('detune1' sets op1, etc)
set op panning ('pan1' sets op1, etc)
set op volume ('pan1' sets op1, etc)

ELSE/pm2~.body: [pm2~] is a 2 operators FM (actually phase modulation) synthesizer. Each oscillator can modulate itself and each other.

title: pm4~

methods.description: set index ('1to2' sets op1 to op2 index, etc)
set op ratio ('ratio1' sets op1, etc)
set op detuning ('detune1' sets op1, etc)
set op panning ('pan1' sets op1, etc)
set op volume ('pan1' sets op1, etc)

ELSE/pm4~.body: [pm~] is a 4 operators FM (actually phase modulation) synthesizer. Each oscillator can modulate itself and each other.

title: pm6~

methods.description: set index ('1to2' sets op1 to op2 index, etc)
set op ratio ('ratio1' sets op1, etc)
set op detuning ('detune1' sets op1, etc)
set op panning ('pan1' sets op1, etc)
set op volume ('pan1' sets op1, etc)

ELSE/pm6~.body: [pm6~] is a 4 operators FM (actually phase modulation) synthesizer. Each oscillator can modulate itself and each other.

title: pm~

arguments.description: modulation frequency in hertz or ratio

methods.description: non zero sets to ratio mode

ELSE/pm~.body: [pm~] is a very basic and simple phase modulation unit, which consists of a pair of sinusoidal oscillators, where one modulates the phase input of another.

title: pol2car

messages.description: converts the last received coordinates pair
phase (in radians) from the polar coordinates
real part of the Cartesian form
imaginary part of the Cartesian form

ELSE/pol2car.body: [pol2car] converts polar coordinates (amplitude / phase) to Cartesian coordinates (real / imaginary).

title: pol2car~

messages.description: amplitude of the signal in the polar form
phase (in radians) of the signal in the polar form
real part of the signal in the Cartesian form
imaginary part of the signal in the Cartesian form

ELSE/pol2car~.body: [pol2car~] converts polar coordinates (amplitude / phase) to Cartesian coordinates (real / imaginary).

title: polymetro

messages.description: syncs (restarts count) when polymetro is on
multiplier as a float or fraction (anything/symbol)

flags.name: -b
flags.description: sets to bang mode output (instead of floats)

ELSE/polymetro.body: [polymetro] is a polyrhythmic metronome. Polyrhythms are better specified with fractions, but you can set it as floats, which is a tempo multiplier value in the end. The left output is a counter for the base tempo count while the right outlet is the relative count/tempo as specified by the multiplier ratio. This is an abstraction based on synced [clock] objects.

title: polymetro~

messages.description: multiplier as a float or fraction (anything/symbol/float)

ELSE/polymetro~.body: [polymetro~] is a polyrhythmic metronome for signal rate that outputs impulses. Polyrhythms are better specified with fractions, but you can set it as floats, which is a tempo multiplier value in the end. The left output is a counter for the base tempo count while the right outlet is the relative count/tempo as specified by the ratio. This is an abstraction based on [pimp~] and [pimpmul~] objects.

title: popmenu

flags.name: -load <float>
flags.description:
sets load value, no float sets current
default: 0

flags.name: -bg <list>
flags.description:
sets background color in hexdecimal or RGB list
default: #dfdfdf

flags.name: -fg <list>
flags.description:
sets foreground color in hexdecimal or RGB list
default: #000000

flags.name: -var <symbol>
flags.description:
assigns a variable to the menu
default: empty

flags.name: -param <symbol>
flags.description:
assigns a parameter to the menu
default: empty

methods.description: sets the width of the menu
sets the height of the menu
adds an item to the menu
sets the receive symbol for messages
sets the send symbol for messages
assigns a variable to the menu
assigns a parameter to the menu
keeps the menu open after selection
loads a list of menu items
triggers when the object is loaded

ELSE/popmenu.body: [popup] is a popup menu GUI. When you click on it, you get a popup menu to choose elements from (symbols or floats). You can choose by clicking on the selected item or via up/down arrow keys and hitting 'enter'. By default, the output is the index, but can also be the element or both.

title: power~

messages.description: the signal raised to the given power

ELSE/power~.body: [power~] is a power function waveshaper for signals that extends the usual definition of exponentiation and returns -pow(-a, b) when you have a negative signal input. This allows not only the output of negative values but also the exponentiation of negative values by non-integer exponents.

title: presets

messages.description: sets a preset number and loads it
save preset (same as 'save' without a float)
presets as a list when receiving "export" message

flags.name: -file <symbol>
flags.description:
set a file to import from
default: none

flags.name: -morph <f, f>
flags.description:
sets morph time and grain
default: 0 20

flags.name: -interp
flags.description:
sets to interpolation mode
default: no interpolation

flags.name: -exp <float>
flags.description:
sets exponential factor for morph/interpolation
default: 1

methods.description: load from a preset number. If no float is given, the last set preset number is loaded
save to a preset number. If no float is given, the last set preset is saved
sets morph time and grain for morphing between presets
clear data from preset number, no float clears all presets
open the preset window so you can see/edit values
import presets from a file, no symbol opens dialog window
write presets to file, no symbol opens dialog window

ELSE/presets.body: [presets] manages presets for patches and abstractions and has interpolation and morphing features. Change the number and symbol below, save a preset and the patch, reopen to recall the presets. Use the "read" message to restore to this example's default.

title: properties

messages.description: bang when asked for properties in the parent patch

ELSE/properties.body: [properties] sends a bang when you put it in an abstraction or a subpatch and ask for properties on the parent patch (by selecting the "properties" option after right clicking). This is useful for making properties for graphical subpatches or abstractions.

title: ptouch.in

flags.name: -ext
flags.description:
only listen to external input source
default: no

methods.description: non zero sets to listen only to external input source

ELSE/ptouch.in.body: [ptouch.in] extracts MIDI polyphonic aftertouch information from raw MIDI input (such as from [midiin]).

title: ptouch.out
ELSE/ptouch.out.body: [ptouch.out] formats and sends raw MIDI polyphonic aftertouch messages. An argument sets channel number (the default is 1).

title: pulse
ELSE/pulse.body: This is much like the [pulse~] oscillator but operates in a control rate (with a default resolution of 1 ms) and the output is like a toggle switch.

title: pulsecount~

messages.description: an impulse resets counter to zero

ELSE/pulsecount~.body: [pulsecount~] counts pulse triggers. A trigger happens at transitions from non positive to positive. A trigger in the right inlet resets the counter to zero. The argument sets a maximum count value (if greater than 0).

title: pulsediv~

messages.description: an impulse resets the counter to the start value
impulse for the other trigger inputs

ELSE/pulsediv~.body: [pulsediv~] outputs impulses when receiving triggers (signal changes from non-positive to positive). The left outlet outputs impulses according to the division value and the right outlet outputs impulses for the other trigger inputs.

title: pulse~

flags.name: -midi
flags.description: sets frequency input in MIDI pitch

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: nonzero sets frequency input in MIDI pitch
<channel, freq> set a single frequency channel
nonzero sets to soft sync mode

ELSE/pulse~.body: [pulse~] is a pulse train oscillator (alternates between 1 and 0, or on/off) that accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for pulse width, phase sync and phase modulation.

title: pvoc.freeze~
ELSE/pvoc.freeze~.body: [pvoc.freeze~] is a freeze object based on a phase vocoder.

title: pvoc.live~

messages.description: resets to the beginning of the delay line

ELSE/pvoc.live~.body: [pvoc.live~] is like [pvoc.player~], but for live input. It provides independent time stretching and pitch shifting via granulation.

title: pvoc.player~

messages.description: (re)start playing the buffer from the beginning
<1> is the same as "start", <0> is the same as "stop
the buffer signal of the corresponding channel
a bang when finishing playing the buffer

arguments.default: 1 if no file is given, or sound file's if given

arguments.description: the name of the file to open

flags.name: -loop
flags.description:
turns loop mode on
default: off

methods.description: opens a file with the symbol name (no symbol opens dialog box) and starts playing
stops and goes back to the beginning
reloads the file into the buffer and starts playing
sets a file to open (needs a reload message)
unpauses and continues playing the buffer

ELSE/pvoc.player~.body: [pvoc.player~] is like [player~] but provides independent time stretching and pitch shifting via a phase vocoder. NOTE: THIS OBJECT IS HEAVY ON THE CPU!!!

title: pz2coeff

messages.description: set coordinates of 2 poles and calculate output
set overall gain of the filter response
biquad coefficients as biquad~ takes it

methods.description: set overall gain of the filter response

ELSE/pz2coeff.body: [pz2coeff] converts an input of 2 poles/zeros coordinates to biquad coefficients.

title: quad~
ELSE/quad~.body:
[quad~] generates a chaotic signal from the difference equation;
y[n] = a * y[n-1]^2 + b * y[n-1] + c

title: quantizer

messages.description: a list of floats to be quantized

ELSE/quantizer.body: [quantizer] approximates a value to step values defined by the argument. There are 4 approximation modes: round (default), int, floor, ceil or both.

title: quantizer~
ELSE/quantizer~.body: [quantizer~] approximates a value to step values defined by the argument. There are 4 approximation modes: round (default), int, floor, ceil or both.

title: rad2deg
ELSE/rad2deg.body: [rad2deg] converts radians (0 to 2*pi) to radians (0 to 360). Besides 0 to 2*pi you can also convert negative values from 0 to -2*pi (0 to -360) - and input values are wrapped if the exceed this input range. If you give it the '-pos' flag, which wraps to positive values only.

title: rad2hz
ELSE/rad2hz.body:
Use [rad2hz] to convert a signal representing a frequency in "Radians per Sample" to Hz. This depends on the patch's sample rate (sr). The conversion formula is;
hz = rad * sr / 2pi

title: rampnoise

messages.description: ramp noise's waveform in the range from 0 - 127

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/rampnoise.body: [rampnoise] is a control rate ramp noise. It doesn't need the DSP on to function. Give it a seed value if you want a reproducible output.

title: rampnoise~

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal
<channel, freq> set a single density channel

ELSE/rampnoise~.body:
[rampnoise~] is a low frequency (band limited) noise generator with interpolation, therefore it ramps from one random value to another, resulting in random ramps. Random values are between -1 and 1 at a rate in Hz (negative frequencies accepted). Use the seed message if you want a reproducible output.
The [rampnoise~] object produces frequencies limited to a band from 0 up to the frequency it is running. It can go up to the sample rate, and when that happens, it's basically a white noise generator.

title: ramp~

messages.description: signal trigger (starts ramp from the reset point value)
control rate trigger (starts ramp from the reset point value)
a bang when ramp is finished

flags.name: -reset
flags.description: stops and goes back to the reset point

methods.description: prevents the ramp from automatically starting

ELSE/ramp~.body: [ramp~] is a resettable linear ramp between a minimum and maximum value. You can trigger it with a bang or with a trigger signal (non-positive to positive transition).

title: rand.dist

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/rand.dist.body: [rand.dist] is an abstraction based on [array quantile] and generates random numbers that follow a probability density function (given by an array). A float input (like from an external random source) outputs the array quantile instead. The first argument is the table name containing the probability density function. The output range is from 0 to 127 by default, but you can change that with the 2nd and 3rd argument or the 2nd or 3rd inlet. The output resolution is the range divided by the table maximum index (127 in the example below, so the 0-127 range outputs integers).

title: rand.f

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/rand.f.body: [rand.f] generates random float values for a given range when triggered with a bang. Use the seed message if you want a reproducible output.

title: rand.f~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/rand.f~.body: [rand.f~] generates random float values for a given range when triggered. A trigger happens at positive to negative or negative to positive transitions. Use the seed message if you want a reproducible output.

title: rand.hist

messages.description: sets new histogram and clears memory

flags.name: -eq <float>
flags.description:
sets equal number of occurrences for all indexes
default: 0

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal
set index and increase occurrence by 1
set index and decrease occurrence by 1
clears the memory and sets a new 'n' size
sets an equal number of occurrences for all elements

ELSE/rand.hist.body: [rand.hist] generates weighted random numbers based on a histogram (which specifies how many times an index is output). Below to the left we have a random sequence where 0 has a 30% chance of occurring, 1 has 20% and 2 has 50%. A list sets a new histogram. In "unrepeat" mode, a random sequence is output without repetition, below to the right, the random sequence contains index 0 three times and index 1 twice. When the sequence is finished, a bang comes out the right outlet. In this case, the 'restart' message clears the memory so you can start a new sequence.

title: rand.i

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/rand.i.body: [rand.i] generates random integer values for a given range when triggered with as bang.Use the seed message if you want a reproducible output.

title: rand.i~

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/rand.i~.body: [rand.i~] generates random integer values for a given range when triggered. A trigger happens at positive to negative or negative to positive transitions. Use the seed message if you want a reproducible output.

title: rand.list

messages.description: choose an element from a given list randomly

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/rand.list.body: [rand.list] takes a list of floats and randomly chooses from it.

title: rand.u

messages.description: clears memory and sets a new size

methods.description: a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal
clears the memory and sets a new 'n' size

ELSE/rand.u.body: [rand.u] generates an unrepeated random values (from 0 to size-1). After the whole sequence is output, a bang is sent in the right outlet.

title: randpulse

methods.description: non-0 sets to random gate value mode

ELSE/randpulse.body: [randpulse] is a random pulse oscillator (which alternates between a random value and 0, or on/off) at control rate with a default resolution of 1 ms). It accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for pulse width and sync.

title: randpulse2

arguments.description: non-0 sets to random gate value mode

methods.description: non-0 sets to random gate value mode
- a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal

ELSE/randpulse2.body: [randpulse2] is a random pulse train oscillator (which alternates between 0 and 1 at random intervals according to a "density" parameter, which controls the average frequency.

title: randpulse2~

arguments.description: non-0 sets to random gate value mode

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to random gate value mode
a float sets seed, no float sets a unique internal
<channel, freq> set a single density channel

ELSE/randpulse2~.body: [randpulse2~] is a random pulse train oscillator. It alternates at random intervals according to a "density" parameter, which controls the average frequency.

title: randpulse~

flags.name: -mc <list>
flags.description: sets multichannel output with a list of frequencies

methods.description: non-0 sets to random gate value mode
<channel, freq> set a single density channel

ELSE/randpulse~.body: [randpulse~] is a random pulse train oscillator (which alternates between a random value and 0, or on/off). It accepts negative frequencies, has inlets for pulse width and sync.

title: range.hsl

messages.description: sets range values and outputs it

methods.description: sets mode (0 - shift, 1 - select, 2 - expand, 3 - expand)

ELSE/range.hsl.body: [range.hsl] is a range horizontal slider GUI abstraction, (based on an object by .mmb). It has 4 modes of operation, see below.

title: range
ELSE/range.body: [range~] analyxes a float input's range values.

title: range~
ELSE/range~.body: [range~] analyzes and outputs the signal's range (minimum and maximum) values.

title: ratio2cents

messages.description: convert or output the last converted value (only float)

methods.description: sets next value to be converted via bang (only float)

ELSE/ratio2cents.body:
Use [ratio2cents] to convert intervals described as rational numbers (expressed as float point decimal values) to intervals in cents. The conversion formula is;
cents = log2(rational) * 1200

title: ratio2cents~
ELSE/ratio2cents~.body:
Use [ratio2cents~] to convert a signal representing a rational interval to an interval in cents. The conversion formula is;
cents = log2(ratio) * 1200

ELSE/rec.file~.body: [rec.file~] is a convenient abstraction based on [writesf~], so it records sample files that you can load with other objects, such as [sample~], [play.file~], [player~] and others.

title: receiver

messages.description: any received messages (or receive names at bangs)

ELSE/receiver.body: [receiver] is like vanilla's [receive]. It can have up to two names, has an inlet to set the receive names and at load time can expand dollar symbols according to parent patches.

title: remove
ELSE/remove.body: [remove] removes one or more number elements from a list. The right inlet or arguments set the elements to remove.

title: repeat~
ELSE/repeat~.description: copy an input signal to multiple channels

flags.name: -font <symbol>
flags.description: default 'dejavu sans mono' or 'menlo' for mac

methods.description: appends a message to the note

ELSE/repeat~.body: [repeat~] takes an input signal of any channel size and repeats it 'n' times.

title: replace
ELSE/replace.body: [replace] replaces an element from an input message according to an element number defined by the 1st argument or mid inlet. Elements are indexed from 1, a 0 value means no element is replaced and negative values count from last to the first element. Element numbers get clipped to the first and last items of the input message. The right inlet or 2nd argument sets the new element.

title: rescale

methods.description: sets the exponential factor, -1, 0 or 1 sets to linear
nonzero sets clipping on, 0 sets it off
nonzero sets to reverse log mode

ELSE/rescale.body: By default, [rescale] rescales MIDI input values from 0 to 127 (including floats) into another range of values (0-1 by default). You can also set to log or exponential factor (0 by default - linear).

title: rescale~

messages.description: value to perform the scaling function on

methods.description: sets the exponential factor, -1, 0 or 1 sets to linear
non zero sets clipping on, 0 sets it off
non zero sets to log mode
non zero sets to reverse log mode

ELSE/rescale~.body: By default, [rescale~] rescales input values from -1 to 1 into another range of values (0-1 by default).  You can also set to log or exponential factor (0 by default - linear).

title: resonant~

messages.description: signal to be filtered or excite the resonator
sets resonance parameter in decay time in ms
sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves

methods.description: sets resonance parameter to bandwidth in octaves
sets resonance parameter to decay time in ms

ELSE/resonant~.body: [resonant~] is a bandpass resonator filter like [bandpass~], but it doesn't have a maximum dB value of 0, so changing the Q increases the gain of the filter. Besides 'Q' and 'bw' you can also set the resonance as 't60' (like with [resonator~]).

title: resonator2~
ELSE/resonator2~.body: [resonator2~] is a wrap around [cpole~]. It is very similar to [vcf~], which is also a wrap around [cpole~], but has no constant maximum gain at 0dB - so it works like [resonator~] with a constant skirt. Like [resonator~], it takes a decay time for resonance. Unlike [resonant~], you shouldn't excite it with noise as the filter produces very high gain output, so it's really suited just to be excited by impulses. By the way, this filter is the basis of [damp.osc~] and [resonbank2~].

title: resonator~
ELSE/resonator~.body: [resonator~] is just like [resonant~], but the resonance parameter is defined as resonance time in 't60'. It has 3 modes, the default is bandpass, which is like [resonant~], then you can also have lowpass (like [lowpass~]) and highpass (you guessed it, like [highpass~]). Note that a t60 value of 0 bypasses the input! For a bank of [resonator~] objects, see [resonbank~]. For another similar filter, see [resonator2~].

title: resonbank2~

messages.description: real part of the resonator bank

flags.name: -partial <list>
flags.description: list of frequencies for all filters

flags.name: -decay <list>
flags.description: list of decay times for all filters

flags.name: -amp <list>
flags.description: list of amplitudes for all filters

flags.name: -ramp <list>
flags.description: list of ramp times for all filters

flags.name: -rampall <float>
flags.description: sets ramp time for all filters

methods.description: sets list of partials for the resonators's frequencies
list of decay times for all filters
list of amplitudes for all filters
list of ramp times for all filters
sets ramp time for all filters

ELSE/resonbank2~.body: [resonbank2~] is a bank of resonators made of [resonator2~] objects. The design and structure here is different than [resonator2~] in order to make it better suited for sound synthesis. It has the same design as [resonbank~] and has a list of partials for each resonator and a frequency multiplier in the left inlet, kinda like [oscbank~]. The mid inlet the trigger (or excitation) signal (kinda like [pluck~]) - this must be an impulse since noise sources and noise bursts generate very loud outputs! The rightmost inlet is a time multiplier for the decay times. The number of filters is set via the parameter list size (such as the partial list). There's also support for multichannel output.

ELSE/resonbank~.body: [resonbank~] is a bank of resonators made of [resonator~] objects. The design and structure here is different than [resonator~] in order to make it better suited for sound synthesis. It has a list of partials for each resonator and a frequency multiplier in the left inlet, kinda like [oscbank~]. The mid inlet the trigger (or excitation) signal (kinda like [pluck~]) and the rightmost inlet is a time multiplier for the decay times. The number of filters is set via the parameter list size (such as the partial list). There's also support for multichannel output.

title: retrieve
ELSE/retrieve.body: [retrieve] retrieves values from things that are connected to a "receive" symbol that matches the argument. It works with [receive], [receiver] and built-in receives in GUIs. In a sense, it uses "receive" names as a "send" as well.

title: retune
ELSE/retune.body: [retune] receives a scale as a list of steps in cents and a base MIDI pitch value (default 60). It then remaps the incoming MIDI pitches (as integers) and retunes the pitches above or below the base value according to the scale.

title: revdelay~

messages.description: signal input into the delay line

ELSE/revdelay~.body: [revdelay~] is a reverse delay effect. It takes a size amount in ms to play backwards from a delay line (default 1000 ms).

title: rint
ELSE/rint.body: [rint] takes floats and rounds them to the nearest integer value.

title: rint~
ELSE/rint~.body: [rint~] takes a float input and rounds it to the nearest integer value.

title: rms~

flags.name: -lin
flags.description:
sets output to linear
default: dBFS

methods.description: sets window and hop size in samples
non zero sets to linear output

ELSE/rms~.body: [rms~] is similar to Pd Vanilla's [env~], but it reports the RMS value in linear amplitude (default) or in dBFS.

title: rm~
ELSE/rm~.body: [rm~] is a Ring Modulation abstraction. It takes a modulation frequency input as the argument or via the control right inlet. O modulation frequency of 0 Hz bypasses the input signal.

title: rotate
ELSE/rotate.body: [rotate] rotates messages/lists. A rotation number sets the offset. If it's positive, the list is shifted to the right. If negative, to the left.

title: rotate.mc~
ELSE/rotate.mc~.body: [rotate.mc~] performs equal power rotation for any number of channels given by a multichannel signal input. It takes a position control in which a full cycle is from 0 to 1, negative values from 0 to -1 are wrapped.

title: rotate~
ELSE/rotate~.body: [rotate~] performs equal power rotation for 'n' channels (default 2). It takes a position control in which a full cycle is from 0 to 1, negative values from 0 to -1 are wrapped.

title: route2

messages.description: any message to be routed according to its 1st element
rest of message that corresponds to an argument
input message if no match was found

arguments.description: list of addresses to route to

ELSE/route2.body: [route2] is similar to route, but it doesn't trim the list selector and always outputs messages with a proper selector. Another difference is that you can mix floats and symbols as arguments. Each argument creates a corresponding outlet and there's an extra rightmost outlet for messages that don't match the arguments.

title: routeall

messages.description: any message to be routed completely
if an input message matches an argument, the corresponding outlet sends that message

arguments.description: arguments to match the 1st element of a message

ELSE/routeall.body:
[routeall] routes messages and - unlike [route] - keeps its first element! Also differently from [route], the [routeall] object matches a symbol to a symbol or a list message! Another difference is that [routeall] takes both float and symbol messages at once! Moreover, it can match to other elements in the message than the first.
Each argument has its corresponding outlet. The object searches for a match between the first element of a message and an argument (from first to last). As soon as a match is found, it sends the message to the corresponding output - this means that it stops looking for other matches. If no match is found, the message is sent to the rightmost outlet.

title: router

messages.description: message to send through a specified outlet
outlets for routing any received message

ELSE/router.body: [router] routes a message from the left inlet to an outlet number specified by the float into the right inlet (if the number is out of range, the message is not output).

title: routetype

messages.description: any message to be routed according to its type
any message that corresponds to a type
non-corresponding messages if less than the 6 types are given

arguments.description: list of types to route; no args — everything is sent to the outlet

ELSE/routetype.body:
[routetype] routes messages according to its type, such as "bang", "float", "list" and "symbol". There is also the "anything" type, which is can be defined as "any other" message that is not the former ones.
These 5 types are given as arguments, and there's an outlet corresponding to each given argument that sends out the message type according to the arguments(bang", "float", "list", "symbol" or "any").

title: sample~

ELSE/sample~.body: [sample~] is an abstraction that creates an audio buffer which you can use to load a sample or record into. It can load and save multichannel files.

messages.description: time value in number of samples

ELSE/samps2ms~.body: [samps2ms~] is a simple abstraction that converts time values number of samples to ms. Conversion expression: ms = 1000*samps/samplerate.

ELSE/separate.body: [separate] allows you to separate a symbol into a list with different elements by setting the character that separates one element from the others. The default separator is a "space".

title: sequencer

arguments.description: list of elements sets the sequence

methods.description: goes to a position index in the sequence (from 1)

ELSE/sequencer.body: [sequencer] sends an element from a given sequence when banged. Elements joined by "-" are sent at the same time but separately (allowing chords). A single "-" is treated as a rest. A "_" is ignored and can be thought as "tie". A "|" represents a barline. Symbol elements are sent without a symbol selector (and can be note names or anything else). A "bang" comes out as a proper "bang".

title: setdsp~
ELSE/setdsp~.body:
[setdsp~] is a convenient abstraction to display and set Pd's audio engine (aka 'DSP') state. You can just click on it to change it to On/Off via its toggle interface. This object is commonly found in the documentation of the ELSE library audio objects, at the top right corner.
By default, the [setdsp~] object will check the current DSP state and load accordingly (on or off). But you can give it an argument to set it on/off.

title: sfinfo

[shaper~] performs waveshaping with transfer functions, in which signal input values (from -1 to 1) are mapped to the the transfer function's indexes. Values outside the -1 to 1 range are wrapped inside it.
You can set an array for [shaper~], as in this example, or use its internal transfer function for Chebyshev polynomials.

ELSE/sh~.body: [sh~] samples and holds a value from the left inlet. In "gate" mode (default), values are sampled if the signal in the right inlet is greater than the threshold (0 by default), or held if lesser or equal to it. In "trigger" mode, the value needs to fall below the threshold before it can retrigger [sh~]. A bang also triggers it.

title: slew

flags.name: -rate <f>
flags.description:
sets refresh rate in ms (minimum 1)
default: 5

methods.description: sets new start point and goes back to target

ELSE/slew.body: [slew] takes an amplitude limit per second and an incoming value to be 'slew limited'. It then generates a line towards the incoming value. The difference between [slew] and [line] is that [slew] has a fixed speed, not a fixed period. A limit of zero stops the line generation and a negative value turns the limiter off.

title: slew2

flags.name: -rate <float>
flags.description:
sets refresh rate in ms (minimum 1)
default: 5

methods.description: sets new start point and goes back to target

ELSE/slew2.body: [slew2] is a slew limiter like [slew], but has a different speed for up and down changes. It takes an amplitude limit per second and an incoming value to be 'slew limited'. It then generates a line towards the incoming value. The difference between [slew] and [line] is that [slew] has a fixed speed, not a fixed period. A limit of zero stops the line generation and a negative value turns the limiter off.

title: slew2~

methods.description: sets new start point and goes back to target

ELSE/slew2~.body: [slew2~] is like [slew~] but has independent values for upwards & downwards ramps. It takes an amplitude limit per second and generates a line towards the incoming value. The difference between [slew~] and [glide~] is that [slew~] has a fixed speed, not a fixed period. A limit of zero stops the line generation and a negative value turns the limiter off (but again, up and down limits are independent).

title: slew~

methods.description: sets new start point and goes back to target

ELSE/slew~.body: [slew~] takes an amplitude limit per second and an incoming value to be 'slew limited'. It then generates a line towards the incoming value. The difference between [slew~] and [glide~] is that [slew~] has a fixed speed, not a fixed period. A limit of zero stops the line generation and a negative value turns the limiter off.

title: slice

messages.description: the left part of the split list
the right part of the split list

ELSE/slice.body: [slice] splits lists. The 'n' split point is set via argument or right inlet. If you slice at 'n', the left outlet spits the first 'n' elements and the right outlet sends the remaining elements. If you slice at '-n', the right outlet spits the last 'n' elements and the left outlet sends the first elements. If the number of elements is less than the split point, the whole list is output to the left if 'n' is positive, and to the right if negative. Symbols and floats are also taken.

title: slice~

messages.description: the left output of the sliced signal
the righft output of the sliced signal

ELSE/slice~.body: [slice~] splits a multichannel signal. The 'n' split point is set via argument or float input. If you slice at 'n', the left outlet spits the first 'n' channels and the right outlet sends the remaining channels. If you slice at '-n', the right outlet spits the last 'n' channels and the left outlet sends the first channels. If the number of channels is less than the split point, the whole signal is output to the left if 'n' is positive (and the right outlet outputs zeros), and to the right if negative (and the left outlet outputs zeros).

title: smooth2~
